Certainty of intention.front: What is the test for certainty of intention to create a trust?
Certainty of intention.answer: The court asks whether the settlor used words or conduct that objectively demonstrate an intention to impose a legally binding obligation on the trustee to hold property for the beneficiary. No particular form of words is required: Paul v Constance [1977]. Precatory words ('I wish', 'I hope', 'I desire') are insufficient unless context compels a mandatory reading: Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884). ⚠️ The court applies an objective test — the settlor's subjective intention is irrelevant.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF INTENTION · OBJECTIVE TEST · PRECATORY WORDS · PAUL V CONSTANCE

Certainty of intention.front: Why did the court find a trust in Paul v Constance [1977] despite informal language?
Certainty of intention.answer: Mr Constance repeatedly told Ms Paul that the money in a bank account was 'as much yours as mine'. The Court of Appeal held this informal but unambiguous statement, viewed in context, was sufficient to manifest an intention to hold the account on trust for both parties equally. The absence of technical vocabulary does not prevent a trust arising if the surrounding circumstances make the obligation clear.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: PAUL V CONSTANCE · INFORMAL LANGUAGE · OBJECTIVE INTENTION · JOINT BENEFICIAL INTEREST

Certainty of intention.front: What are precatory words and why do they fail to create a trust?
Certainty of intention.answer: Precatory words express a hope, wish, or desire rather than a command: 'I wish my wife to provide for my children', 'I request', 'I desire'. They fail because they do not impose a legally binding obligation — the recipient is free to comply or not. Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884): 'in full confidence that she will do what is right' held insufficient. The modern court will not strain to find a trust from mere moral exhortation.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: PRECATORY WORDS · NO BINDING OBLIGATION · RE ADAMS · MORAL EXHORTATION ≠ TRUST

Certainty of intention.front: A testator leaves property 'to my daughter, knowing she will look after her brother.' Is a trust created?
Certainty of intention.answer: Almost certainly not. The phrase 'knowing she will look after' is precatory — it expresses confidence but imposes no legal obligation. Following Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884), such language does not satisfy certainty of intention. The daughter takes the property absolutely. ⚠️ Trap: the testator's obvious intention to benefit the brother is irrelevant if the words chosen are merely expressive of hope rather than command.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: PRECATORY WORDS · ABSOLUTE GIFT · RE ADAMS · SUBJECTIVE INTENTION IRRELEVANT

Certainty of intention.issue: Whether informal words and conduct could constitute a sufficient declaration of trust over a bank account, satisfying certainty of intention.
Certainty of intention.rule: Certainty of intention does not require the word 'trust'. It is satisfied where the settlor's words or conduct, assessed objectively, show an unequivocal intention to create a trust rather than make a gift or impose a mere moral obligation.
Certainty of intention.application: Mr Constance repeatedly told his partner 'this money is as much yours as mine' in relation to a deposit account funded partly by a damages award. The court held these words, in context, sufficiently evidenced an intention to hold the fund on trust for both parties equally.
Certainty of intention.conclusion: A trust can be created without technical language. Words demonstrating shared beneficial ownership, consistently used, will satisfy certainty of intention. Critical for informal family and cohabitation arrangements.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF INTENTION · INFORMAL WORDS · NO MAGIC FORMULA · OBJECTIVE TEST

Certainty of intention.front: Gerald transfers £10,000 to his daughter Sophie and tells her: 'I want you to look after this for your brother Tom until he turns 21.' Sophie understands the money is for Tom's benefit. Does a trust arise?
Certainty of intention.answer: Yes. Gerald's words demonstrate a clear intention to create a trust, not to make an outright gift to Sophie. The language ('look after this for Tom') objectively indicates that Sophie is to hold the money for Tom's benefit rather than her own. There is no requirement that the word 'trust' be used: Paul v Constance [1977]. The three certainties are satisfied: intention (Gerald's words), subject matter (£10,000), and objects (Tom). The trust is validly created.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF INTENTION · INFORMAL WORDS · TRUST NOT GIFT · PAUL V CONSTANCE

Certainty of intention.front: A will states: 'I leave my residuary estate to my wife, confident that she will provide for our children as she sees fit.' The wife wishes to keep the entire estate for herself. Can the children enforce any claim?
Certainty of intention.answer: No. Precatory words — expressions of hope, confidence or desire — do not create a trust. 'Confident that she will' expresses a moral expectation, not a legal obligation. The court distinguishes mandatory language (which creates trusts) from precatory language (which does not): Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884). The wife takes the estate outright and is under no legal obligation to the children. The children have no enforceable equitable claim.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF INTENTION · PRECATORY WORDS · NO TRUST · RE ADAMS

Certainty of intention.front: Viktor opens a joint bank account with his daughter Anna. He tells her: 'I want this to be our emergency fund — it's for both of us.' He deposits £20,000 of his own money. Viktor dies. His estate claims the full £20,000. Anna claims half. Advise.
Certainty of intention.answer: A resulting trust analysis applies. Viktor provided all the funds, so prima facie he holds the beneficial interest (Dyer v Dyer (1788)). The question is whether his words ('for both of us') constitute a sufficient declaration of trust in Anna's favour. Following Paul v Constance [1977], informal words indicating shared ownership can satisfy certainty of intention. However, 'emergency fund — for both of us' is ambiguous: it may indicate practical access rather than beneficial ownership. A court would examine the whole course of conduct. If Viktor intended Anna to share beneficially, a 50/50 trust is established. If the joint account was merely for convenience, a resulting trust returns the full sum to his estate. The words alone are borderline — the outcome depends on further evidence of intention.
Certainty of intention.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF INTENTION · JOINT ACCOUNT · RESULTING TRUST · PAUL V CONSTANCE

Certainty of subject matter.front: What does certainty of subject matter require, and what happens if it is absent?
Certainty of subject matter.answer: The trust property must be identifiable with sufficient precision for the trustee to know exactly what to hold and for the court to enforce the trust. Two limbs: (1) the trust property itself must be certain; (2) each beneficiary's share must be certain. If either limb fails, the purported trust fails and the property is held on resulting trust for the settlor (or falls into residue on death).
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF SUBJECT MATTER · TWO LIMBS · PROPERTY + SHARE · RESULTING TRUST ON FAILURE

Certainty of subject matter.front: 'The bulk of my estate to my children on trust.' Does this satisfy certainty of subject matter?
Certainty of subject matter.answer: No. 'Bulk' is conceptually uncertain — it does not identify a specific quantum. Palmer v Simmonds (1854): 'the bulk of my residuary estate' failed for want of certainty. The court cannot give effect to a trust it cannot enforce because the property is unidentifiable. The purported trust fails and the property passes absolutely or on resulting trust.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: BULK · CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY · PALMER V SIMMONDS · TRUST FAILS

Certainty of subject matter.front: Can a trust be declared over an unascertained portion of a larger fungible mass (e.g. shares)?
Certainty of subject matter.answer: It depends on whether the assets are tangible or intangible. For tangible goods, segregation is required: Re London Wine Co [1986] — unsegregated wine bottles could not be held on trust. For intangible assets (shares, money in a fund), segregation may not be needed: Hunter v Moss [1994] held a trust of 50 out of 950 identical shares valid because shares are fungible and any 50 are equivalent. ⚠️ The Hunter v Moss principle does not apply to tangible goods.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: FUNGIBILITY · HUNTER V MOSS · SHARES · RE LONDON WINE · TANGIBLES NEED SEGREGATION

Certainty of subject matter.front: What is the distinction between conceptual and evidential uncertainty in the context of subject matter?
Certainty of subject matter.answer: Conceptual uncertainty means the description of the property is inherently vague and cannot be given a precise legal meaning (fatal: trust void). Evidential uncertainty means the property can be defined precisely but it is difficult to prove in fact which items fall within it (not necessarily fatal: courts can apply extrinsic evidence). Example: 'all my blue-chip shares' may be conceptually uncertain; '500 BT plc ordinary shares' is conceptually certain but may require evidence to identify from a mixed holding.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY · EVIDENTIAL UNCERTAINTY · VOID VS PROVABLE · DISTINCTION

Certainty of subject matter.issue: Whether a declaration of trust over 50 shares out of a larger holding of 950 identical shares could satisfy certainty of subject matter, without segregating the specific 50 shares.
Certainty of subject matter.rule: Certainty of subject matter can be satisfied over intangible property (shares of the same class and company) without physical segregation, because each unit is indistinguishable and no injustice results from treating the holding as a whole from which the trust share is drawn.
Certainty of subject matter.application: The defendant declared a trust of 50 of his 950 shares in the same company in favour of the plaintiff. The Court of Appeal distinguished Re London Wine Co (tangible goods) and upheld the trust, finding that with fungible intangibles, segregation is unnecessary.
Certainty of subject matter.conclusion: Hunter v Moss creates an important distinction: segregation is not required for intangible fungible assets (shares of one class). It does not apply to tangible goods. Contrast Re London Wine Co [1986] where physical segregation of bottles was required.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF SUBJECT MATTER · INTANGIBLES · FUNGIBILITY · CONTRAST RE LONDON WINE

Certainty of subject matter.issue: Whether a trust of 'the bulk of my residuary estate' satisfied certainty of subject matter.
Certainty of subject matter.rule: A trust will fail for uncertainty of subject matter if the property cannot be identified with sufficient precision. 'The bulk of' a fund is too vague — bulk connotes a large but unquantified portion, not a defined amount.
Certainty of subject matter.application: The testatrix directed trustees to hold 'the bulk of my residuary estate' on trust. The court held this failed: 'bulk' was an inherently imprecise term incapable of identifying a definite quantum of property.
Certainty of subject matter.conclusion: Subject matter must be defined with precision. Vague quantifiers ('bulk', 'most', 'a reasonable amount') will invalidate the trust. Settlors must specify the exact property or a mechanism for determining it.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF SUBJECT MATTER · BULK · VAGUENESS · TRUST VOID

Certainty of subject matter.front: Harriet declares that she holds 'a reasonable portion' of her wine cellar on trust for her nephew James. James seeks to enforce the trust. Advise him.
Certainty of subject matter.answer: The trust fails for uncertainty of subject matter. A settlor must identify the trust property with sufficient precision: Palmer v Simmonds (1854). 'A reasonable portion' is inherently vague — it does not define a specific quantity, proportion, or mechanism for determination. As there is no ascertainable subject matter, no trust is constituted. James has no enforceable claim. Compare Re London Wine Co [1986]: even where goods are identified by type, segregation of a specific lot is required for tangible property.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF SUBJECT MATTER · VAGUENESS · PALMER V SIMMONDS · TRUST FAILS

Certainty of subject matter.front: Marcus holds 500 ordinary shares in Apex plc. He declares himself trustee of 100 of those shares for his colleague Priya. He does not segregate any specific shares. Is the trust valid?
Certainty of subject matter.answer: Yes. This satisfies certainty of subject matter. The shares are intangible, fungible assets — every share in the same class of Apex plc is identical and interchangeable. The Court of Appeal in Hunter v Moss [1994] held that segregation is not required for intangible fungibles: any 100 shares drawn from the 500 would satisfy the trust equally. Contrast Re London Wine Co [1986], where tangible goods (bottles of wine) required physical segregation because one bottle might differ from another. The 100 Apex shares are sufficiently certain.
Certainty of subject matter.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF SUBJECT MATTER · INTANGIBLE FUNGIBLES · HUNTER V MOSS · NO SEGREGATION NEEDED

Certainty of objects.front: What is the test for certainty of objects for a fixed trust?
Certainty of objects.answer: For a fixed trust (where each beneficiary's share is specified), the complete list test applies: it must be possible to compile a complete list of all beneficiaries: IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]. The court must know exactly who all the beneficiaries are, because fixed shares cannot be distributed without knowing the full class. If the complete list cannot be drawn up, the trust fails for uncertainty of objects.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: FIXED TRUST · COMPLETE LIST TEST · IRC V BROADWAY COTTAGES · ALL BENEFICIARIES KNOWN

Certainty of objects.front: What is the test for certainty of objects for a discretionary trust?
Certainty of objects.answer: The is/is not test from McPhail v Doulton [1971] (House of Lords, reversing Broadway Cottages): the trust is valid if it can be said with certainty whether any given individual is or is not a member of the class. The trustees need not be able to compile a complete list — they must simply be able to determine inclusion or exclusion for any postulant. ⚠️ This is more permissive than the fixed trust test.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: DISCRETIONARY TRUST · IS/IS NOT TEST · McPHAIL V DOULTON · NOT COMPLETE LIST

Certainty of objects.front: A discretionary trust is created for 'my friends'. Will it satisfy the is/is not test?
Certainty of objects.answer: No — 'friends' is conceptually uncertain. The term has no clear legal meaning: different people apply different criteria (social acquaintance vs close friend). Re Barlow's Will Trusts [1979] (Browne-Wilkinson J): 'friends' is too vague for a trust (though workable for a power in certain contexts). Because the court cannot determine whether any given postulant 'is or is not' a friend, the discretionary trust fails. ⚠️ Contrast powers, where the court may take a more liberal approach.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: FRIENDS · CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY · McPHAIL TEST FAILS · RE BARLOW

Certainty of objects.front: What is the difference between conceptual uncertainty and administrative unworkability in discretionary trusts?
Certainty of objects.answer: Conceptual uncertainty: the class description cannot be given a clear meaning (e.g. 'friends') — trust void. Administrative unworkability: the class is conceptually clear but so large that the trustees cannot sensibly exercise a discretion in favour of individuals: R v District Auditor ex p West Yorkshire MCC [1986] — a trust for 'the inhabitants of West Yorkshire' (2.5 million people) void for administrative unworkability. ⚠️ Both are separate grounds of failure — a class can be conceptually clear yet still fail.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY · ADMINISTRATIVE UNWORKABILITY · WEST YORKSHIRE · TWO GROUNDS

Certainty of objects.front: How did Re Baden's Deed Trusts (No 2) [1973] address evidential uncertainty in discretionary trusts?
Certainty of objects.answer: The Court of Appeal unanimously upheld the trust (for employees and relatives) but split on approach: Sachs LJ — evidential uncertainty never vitiates a trust; postulants not proven in suffer exclusion. Megaw LJ — trust valid if a substantial number can be identified with certainty. Stamp LJ — the is/is not test must be satisfied for every postulant; near-certainty insufficient. The case shows evidential uncertainty is generally more tolerable than conceptual uncertainty under McPhail v Doulton.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: RE BADEN NO 2 · EVIDENTIAL UNCERTAINTY · THREE APPROACHES · SACHS · MEGAW · STAMP

Certainty of objects.front: What is the test for certainty of objects of a mere power (as opposed to a trust)?
Certainty of objects.answer: A mere power also applies the is/is not test: Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970] (House of Lords). However, unlike a discretionary trust, failure to exercise a mere power does not result in automatic equal distribution — the power simply lapses. The donee of a power has a discretion to act or not; a trustee of a discretionary trust is under a duty to distribute among the class.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: MERE POWER · IS/IS NOT TEST · RE GULBENKIAN · DISCRETION NOT DUTY · COMPARE TRUST

Certainty of objects.issue: Whether the 'complete list' test for certainty of objects applicable to fixed trusts should also apply to discretionary trusts, or whether a more flexible test was appropriate.
Certainty of objects.rule: The House of Lords (Lord Wilberforce) held that the correct test for discretionary trusts is the same as for powers: whether it can be said with certainty that any given individual is or is not within the class. The 'complete list' test (IRC v Broadway Cottages) was rejected for discretionary trusts.
Certainty of objects.application: Baden's settlement gave trustees discretion to benefit 'employees, ex-employees, relatives and dependants'. Applying the is/is not test, the class was sufficiently certain even though compiling an exhaustive list would be impossible.
Certainty of objects.conclusion: The is/is not test now governs both powers and discretionary trusts. Fixed trusts still require a complete list. McPhail v Doulton liberalised the law, enabling broad discretionary trusts common in modern practice.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF OBJECTS · IS/IS NOT TEST · DISCRETIONARY TRUSTS · OVERRULES BROADWAY COTTAGES

Certainty of objects.front: IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955] CA
Certainty of objects.issue: Whether a discretionary trust could be valid where trustees could not compile a complete list of all potential beneficiaries.
Certainty of objects.rule: A discretionary trust requires that a complete list of all beneficiaries can be drawn up. If the class is so uncertain that a complete list cannot be compiled, the trust fails. This is the 'complete list' test.
Certainty of objects.application: The discretionary trust in favour of a class of employees and their families was held void because the class was insufficiently defined to allow a complete list to be made. The court held equal division was the default mechanism, requiring a complete list.
Certainty of objects.conclusion: Now of largely historical significance for trusts (overruled by McPhail v Doulton for discretionary trusts) but remains the applicable test for fixed trusts, where each beneficiary's share is pre-determined and a complete list is essential.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: COMPLETE LIST TEST · FIXED TRUSTS · OVERRULED FOR DISCRETIONARY TRUSTS BY McPHAIL

Certainty of objects.front: A discretionary trust deed directs trustees to distribute income 'among such of my friends as the trustees shall in their absolute discretion select'. A potential beneficiary, Louise, claims she is within the class. Can she enforce the trust?
Certainty of objects.answer: The trust is likely void for uncertainty of objects. 'Friends' is a conceptually uncertain class: it is impossible to say with certainty whether any given individual is or is not a friend of the settlor — the term has no legally precise meaning. Applying the is/is not test from McPhail v Doulton [1971], the class fails because the concept of friendship is inherently uncertain. Contrast Re Barlow's Will Trusts [1979], where 'friends' was saved in a fixed-share gift context by treating it as a condition precedent. In a discretionary trust, conceptual certainty is required throughout the class — the trust fails and Louise has no claim.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF OBJECTS · FRIENDS · CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY · McPHAIL IS/IS NOT TEST

Certainty of objects.front: A fixed trust directs trustees to divide a fund equally 'among all employees of Novo Ltd who have worked for the company for at least three years'. The company has 4,000 employees globally and HR records are incomplete for overseas staff. Is the trust valid?
Certainty of objects.answer: This is a fixed trust — each qualifying employee receives an equal share — so the complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]) applies. The trustees must be able to compile an exhaustive list of every beneficiary. Incomplete HR records for overseas staff create evidential uncertainty, but this is administrative rather than conceptual uncertainty: the class is defined with precision (employees + 3-year service). Courts can direct enquiries and rely on the best available evidence. Provided a complete list can eventually be compiled (even with difficulty), the trust should be valid. If some employees genuinely cannot be identified, the trustees may pay those shares into court. The trust is not void.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF OBJECTS · FIXED TRUST · COMPLETE LIST TEST · EVIDENTIAL vs CONCEPTUAL UNCERTAINTY

Certainty of objects.front: A discretionary trust gives trustees power to benefit 'such of my relatives and dependants as the trustees think fit'. One potential beneficiary, Nina, is the settlor's cohabitant of five years. She has never been financially dependent on him. Is she within the class?
Certainty of objects.answer: Probably not as a 'dependant'. The is/is not test (McPhail v Doulton [1971]) requires that it can be said with certainty whether any individual is or is not within the class. 'Dependants' requires financial dependency on the settlor. On the facts, Nina has not been financially dependent — she falls outside the class on those grounds. As to 'relatives', this generally means those related by blood or marriage; a cohabitant is not a relative unless adoption or another legal relationship applies. The trustees should take legal advice before including Nina in any distribution. The class itself — relatives and dependants — is sufficiently certain as a concept.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF OBJECTS · IS/IS NOT TEST · DEPENDANTS · RELATIVES · McPHAIL

Certainty of objects.front: Going deeper: Administrative unworkability and the limits of McPhail v Doulton
Certainty of objects.answer: McPhail v Doulton [1971] replaced the complete list test for discretionary trusts with the is/is not test. In Re Baden's Deed Trusts (No 2) [1973], the Court of Appeal applied this test but introduced a further gloss: even if the is/is not test is satisfied, a trust may fail for 'administrative unworkability' if the class is so vast as to be practically incapable of execution. Sachs LJ, Megaw LJ, and Stamp LJ each gave different reasons, creating a notoriously fractured decision. C.T. Emery ('The Most Hallowed Principle', (1982) 98 LQR 551) argued that the administrative unworkability concept is theoretically incoherent: if the is/is not test is satisfied for each individual, it is unclear why the aggregate class size should defeat the trust. The unresolved tension lies between pragmatic judicial manageability (trustees need workable classes) and principled certainty doctrine (the is/is not test should be sufficient). The width of Lord Wilberforce's dicta in McPhail — citing 'all the inhabitants of West Yorkshire' as a potentially unworkable class — has never been definitively applied to invalidate a trust in practice. The question of how wide is too wide remains unanswered in English authority.

Certainty of objects.front: Going deeper: Saunders v Vautier — autonomy, settlor intent, and offshore restriction
Certainty of objects.answer: The rule in Saunders v Vautier (1841) permits adult beneficiaries who are of full capacity and together hold the entire beneficial interest to collapse a trust and demand the legal estate, regardless of the settlor's contrary wishes. Paul Matthews ('The Comparative Importance of the Rule in Saunders v Vautier', (2006) 122 LQR 266) challenged the rule's theoretical basis: he argued it is not a genuine equitable principle but a historically contingent rule that developed before the concept of the trust as a distinct institution was fully formed. On Matthews' analysis, Saunders v Vautier incorrectly prioritises beneficiary autonomy over settlor intent — the settlor created the trust precisely to prevent immediate distribution. Offshore trust legislation has moved against the rule: Jersey's Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984 (as amended) and equivalent legislation in the Cayman Islands permit settlors to exclude the rule entirely, allowing the trust to endure despite unanimous beneficiary demand. English trust law has no equivalent mechanism. The unresolved tension: should English law permit settlors to oust Saunders v Vautier — protecting, for example, spendthrift beneficiaries or asset protection purposes — or does the rule's protection of beneficiary autonomy form part of the irreducible character of the trust that should not be contractable out of?

Certainty of objects.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A settlor creates a trust fund directing trustees to distribute income 'among such of my employees as the trustees shall select in their absolute discretion'. A dispute arises as to whether the trust is valid. You conclude it fails because the trustees cannot compile a complete list of all employees.
Certainty of objects.issue: The Trap: Applying the complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]) to a discretionary trust. The student identifies a trust with a wide discretionary class and defaults to the rule for fixed trusts — that all beneficiaries must be listable — and concludes the trust is void.
Certainty of objects.rule: The Reality: The complete list test was overruled for discretionary trusts by McPhail v Doulton [1971]. The correct test is whether it can be said with certainty that any given individual is or is not within the class (the is/is not test). For 'employees', this test is straightforwardly satisfied — employment status is a binary, legally defined condition. The trust is valid.
Certainty of objects.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The complete list test remains the rule for fixed trusts (where each beneficiary's share is pre-determined). Students conflate the two. The distinction is: discretionary trust → is/is not test; fixed trust → complete list test. The scenario uses 'in their absolute discretion', which is the hallmark of a discretionary trust.
Certainty of objects.conclusion: The Key Rule: Identify the trust type before applying a certainty test. Discretionary trust = McPhail is/is not test. Fixed trust = Broadway Cottages complete list test. 'Absolute discretion' always signals discretionary.
Certainty of objects.summary_line: CERTAINTY OF OBJECTS · IS/IS NOT TEST · McPHAIL · DISCRETIONARY vs FIXED

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: What formalities are required to create an express trust of land inter vivos?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925: a declaration of trust respecting any land or any interest therein must be manifested and proved by some writing signed by some person who is able to declare such trust, or by his will. The declaration need not be in writing at the time — it can be evidenced in writing subsequently. Oral trusts of land are unenforceable, not void. ⚠️ S.53(2): resulting, implied, and constructive trusts are exempt from s.53(1)(b).
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: S.53(1)(B) LPA 1925 · LAND · MANIFESTED IN WRITING · SIGNED · NOT VOID BUT UNENFORCEABLE

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: What formality is required to dispose of an equitable interest under s.53(1)(c) LPA 1925?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: Section 53(1)(c) LPA 1925: a disposition of an equitable interest or trust subsisting at the time of the disposition must be in writing signed by the person disposing of the same, or by his agent thereunto lawfully authorised in writing or by will. Unlike s.53(1)(b), a s.53(1)(c) disposition must be in writing at the time — subsequent written evidence is insufficient. Failure to comply renders the disposition void (not merely unenforceable): Grey v IRC [1960].
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: S.53(1)(C) · EQUITABLE INTEREST · MUST BE IN WRITING · VOID IF NOT · GREY V IRC

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: Does a direction by a beneficiary to trustees to hold on trust for a third party require writing under s.53(1)(c)?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: Yes: Grey v IRC [1960] (House of Lords). Mr Hunter held units on bare trust for himself; he directed the trustees orally to hold for his grandchildren. The HL held this was a disposition of the equitable interest requiring writing under s.53(1)(c). The oral direction was void. ⚠️ The mischief is tax avoidance — if oral directions could pass equitable interests, stamp duty could be evaded. The writing requirement prevents this.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: GREY V IRC · ORAL DIRECTION · EQUITABLE INTEREST · VOID · STAMP DUTY · WRITING REQUIRED

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: Does a contract to assign an equitable interest require writing under s.53(1)(c)?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: No — Oughtred v IRC [1960] (HL, by a narrow majority) and Neville v Wilson [1996] (CA): an oral contract to assign an equitable interest creates a constructive trust over it (s.53(2) exempts constructive trusts from s.53(1)(c)), so the equitable interest passes under the constructive trust without writing. ⚠️ The actual assignment itself still needs writing; it is the specifically enforceable contract that transfers the equitable interest in equity before completion.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: OUGHTRED V IRC · NEVILLE V WILSON · CONTRACT · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · S.53(2) EXEMPTION

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: Clara verbally declares herself trustee of her house for her son Ben. She does not put the declaration in writing. Is the declaration effective?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: No. Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 requires that a declaration of trust of land or any interest in land must be manifested and proved by signed writing. An oral declaration is not sufficient — it must be evidenced in writing. Clara's verbal declaration has no legal effect. The house remains hers beneficially as well as legally. Ben has no enforceable equitable interest unless and until a written, signed declaration is made. Note: unlike contracts for the sale of land, the writing need not create the trust — it must merely evidence it.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: FORMALITIES · s.53(1)(b) LPA 1925 · TRUST OF LAND · WRITING REQUIRED

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: Daniel holds shares on trust for Elena. Elena verbally directs Daniel to transfer her beneficial interest to Fiona. Has the disposition been validly made?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: No. Section 53(1)(c) LPA 1925 requires that a disposition of a subsisting equitable interest must be in writing signed by the disponor or their authorised agent. Elena's verbal direction is void. The equitable interest remains with Elena. The policy rationale is that the legal owner (Daniel) needs to be informed in writing of any change in beneficial ownership — without this, he cannot safely discharge his duties. Elena must execute a written, signed instrument to transfer her equitable interest to Fiona.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: FORMALITIES · s.53(1)(c) LPA 1925 · DISPOSITION OF EQUITABLE INTEREST · WRITING REQUIRED

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front: Maria holds 5,000 shares on trust for Nathan under an express trust. Nathan orally directs Maria to 'declare yourself trustee of those shares for my daughter Olivia instead'. Maria verbally agrees. Has the beneficial interest passed to Olivia?
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).answer: No, for two reasons. First, Nathan's oral direction is a disposition of a subsisting equitable interest (from Nathan to Olivia), which requires writing signed by Nathan under s.53(1)(c) LPA 1925: Grey v IRC [1960]. The oral direction is void. Second, even if the direction were effective, Maria's verbal agreement to hold on trust for Olivia might constitute a sub-trust (which may not require s.53(1)(c) if Nathan retains no interest — Oughtred v IRC [1960]) but Maria's agreement alone cannot create a valid declaration of trust of a subsisting equitable interest without the formalities. The equitable interest remains with Nathan. He must execute a written, signed disposition to transfer it to Olivia.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: s.53(1)(c) · DISPOSITION EQUITABLE INTEREST · WRITING REQUIRED · GREY V IRC

Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A settlor makes an oral declaration of trust over her house for her daughter. Three weeks later, she signs and delivers a written document confirming the declaration. You advise the daughter that the trust is valid because the later writing retrospectively satisfies s.53(1)(b) LPA 1925.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).issue: The Trap: Assuming that a written document executed after an oral declaration can retrospectively cure the lack of formality. The student reasons that s.53(1)(b) requires 'writing' and writing now exists, so the trust must be valid.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).rule: The Reality: Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 requires that a declaration of trust of land must be 'manifested and proved by some writing signed by some person who is able to declare such trust'. The writing must evidence the declaration — but the declaration itself must have been properly evidenced at the time it was made, or a fresh declaration must be made in writing. An oral declaration is not cured by later writing: the original oral declaration has no legal effect. If the later writing constitutes a new, present declaration of trust, that writing is the operative document — but the original oral declaration remains invalid and any intervening dealings (e.g. gifts, death) would not be covered.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).application: Why the Distractor Fails: s.53(1)(b) does not operate retrospectively. It is not equivalent to s.2 Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 for contracts. The writing must evidence a valid declaration — it cannot validate an invalid one. The later document is only effective as a new declaration from its date.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).conclusion: The Key Rule: s.53(1)(b) requires contemporaneous (or prior) written evidence of the declaration. Later writing does not cure an earlier oral declaration. A fresh written declaration is required, effective only from its date.
Formalities (s.53 LPA 1925).summary_line: s.53(1)(b) · FORMALITIES · ORAL DECLARATION · NO RETROSPECTIVE CURE · TRUST OF LAND

Constitution of trusts.front: What is the rule in Milroy v Lord [1862] and why does it matter?
Constitution of trusts.answer: Turner LJ: for a trust or gift to be completely constituted, the settlor must either (1) transfer the property to trustees by the proper method for that type of property ('everything necessary for the transfer has been done'), or (2) declare himself trustee. Equity will not assist a volunteer: if the settlor has done everything necessary and legal title has passed, the trust is constituted. If the transfer is imperfect, equity will not compel completion — it will not 'perfect an imperfect gift': Re Rose [1952] later qualified this.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: MILROY V LORD · COMPLETE CONSTITUTION · TRANSFER OR SELF-DECLARATION · EQUITY WILL NOT ASSIST VOLUNTEER

Constitution of trusts.front: What is the rule in Re Rose [1952] and how does it qualify Milroy v Lord?
Constitution of trusts.answer: The Court of Appeal held that a trust is constituted (and a gift complete) once the donor has done everything within his power to effect the transfer — even if legal title has not yet formally passed. Re Rose: share transfers signed and delivered to the company; before registration the donor died. CA held the gift complete because the donor had done all he could — registration was outside his control. ⚠️ Contrast: if the donor retains a discretion to revoke, the rule does not apply.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: RE ROSE · EVERYTHING IN DONOR'S POWER · COMPLETE BEFORE REGISTRATION · QUALIFIES MILROY

Constitution of trusts.front: How did Pennington v Waine [2002] extend the Re Rose principle?
Constitution of trusts.answer: The CA held that a gift of shares was complete in equity even though the donor had not taken all steps within her control (the transfer form had not been delivered to the company or the donee). The court applied estoppel-based reasoning: the donor had made representations to the donee and it would be unconscionable to revoke. ⚠️ Pennington v Waine is controversial and may represent the outer limit of the doctrine; it should be distinguished from cases where the donor retains full control.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: PENNINGTON V WAINE · ESTOPPEL · UNCONSCIONABLE · EXTENDS RE ROSE · CONTROVERSIAL

Constitution of trusts.front: Can a trust be constituted by the donor declaring himself trustee? What is the risk?
Constitution of trusts.answer: Yes: self-declaration is one of the two modes in Milroy v Lord. No transfer to a third-party trustee is needed. However, courts are vigilant against a failed transfer being re-characterised as a self-declaration: T Choithram International SA v Pagarani [2001] (PC) — a purported gift to a foundation of which the donor was a trustee was treated as a self-declaration. ⚠️ Courts will not allow a donor to elect between modes after a failed transfer: the declaration must be clear at the outset.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: SELF-DECLARATION · T CHOITHRAM · MILROY V LORD · FAILED TRANSFER ≠ SELF-DECLARATION

Constitution of trusts.front: What is the Strong v Bird rule and when does it apply?
Constitution of trusts.answer: The rule in Strong v Bird (1874): an imperfect gift is perfected if (1) the donor intends an immediate gift, (2) that intention continues unchanged until death, and (3) the donee obtains legal title as executor of the donor's estate. The executor's legal title vests the gift. The rule has been extended to administrators: Re James [1935]. ⚠️ The continuing intention requirement is strict: any change of mind destroys the rule.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: STRONG V BIRD · IMPERFECT GIFT · EXECUTOR · CONTINUING INTENTION · RE JAMES

Constitution of trusts.front: What is a donatio mortis causa (DMC) and what are its requirements?
Constitution of trusts.answer: A DMC is a conditional inter vivos gift made in contemplation of impending death, taking full effect only on death. Requirements (Cain v Moon [1896]): (1) made in contemplation of death from a specific expected cause; (2) conditional on death — revocable if the donor survives; (3) delivery of the subject matter or dominion over it. Sen v Headley [1991] confirmed DMC applies to land. ⚠️ DMC perfects an imperfect gift — the donor need not have complied with the Wills Act.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: DONATIO MORTIS CAUSA · CONTEMPLATION OF DEATH · CONDITIONAL · DELIVERY · SEN V HEADLEY

Constitution of trusts.issue: Whether an incompletely constituted trust could be enforced where the settlor had done some but not all acts required to transfer legal title to the trustee.
Constitution of trusts.rule: Turner LJ: equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. A trust is only constituted when legal title in the trust property has been effectively vested in the trustee by the appropriate method for that type of property. An ineffective transfer cannot be saved by construing it as a declaration of trust by the transferor.
Constitution of trusts.application: Samuel Lord held a power of attorney to transfer shares to himself as trustee, but never did so. The court held no trust was constituted: the power of attorney did not itself vest legal title, and the court would not treat the failed transfer as a self-declaration.
Constitution of trusts.conclusion: The fundamental rule: equity will not assist a volunteer by completing an imperfect gift or constituting a failed transfer as a trust. The settlor must do everything within their power to transfer the property. Subject to exceptions in Re Rose and Pennington v Waine.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · EQUITY WILL NOT PERFECT IMPERFECT GIFT · VOLUNTEER · APPROPRIATE METHOD

Constitution of trusts.issue: At what point is a transfer of shares to a trustee complete in equity, where the transferor has done everything required of them but registration by the company has not yet occurred?
Constitution of trusts.rule: Where the transferor has done everything in their power to transfer the property — executed the transfer form and delivered it with the share certificate — equity treats the transfer as complete at that point, even though legal title does not pass until registration.
Constitution of trusts.application: Mr Rose executed share transfers and delivered them to the transferees before his death. The company later registered the transfers. The court held the gifts were complete in equity when the transfers were delivered: Rose had done all he could do.
Constitution of trusts.conclusion: The Re Rose principle: the transferor must have done all acts within their own power to effect the transfer. Once they have, equity treats beneficial ownership as having passed immediately. Legal title follows on registration. Critically limits Milroy v Lord.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · DONE EVERYTHING IN POWER · EQUITY COMPLETE · REGISTRATION FOLLOWS

Constitution of trusts.issue: Whether an incompletely constituted gift of shares could be enforced where, although the donor had not delivered the transfer form, it would be unconscionable to allow the donor to resile from the gift.
Constitution of trusts.rule: Clarke LJ: equity may treat an imperfect gift as complete where it would be unconscionable for the donor to recall it, even if the donor has not done all acts within their power. This is a controversial extension of Re Rose, justified by detrimental reliance or representation.
Constitution of trusts.application: Ada Crampton signed a share transfer form appointing Harold Pennington as director but the form was never delivered. Harold had agreed to act and was told the shares were his. The court held it unconscionable to allow Ada's estate to resile: the gift was complete in equity.
Constitution of trusts.conclusion: Pennington v Waine introduces an unconscionability exception to Milroy v Lord. Criticised as unprincipled but remains good law. Applies where the donee has relied on the gift or the donor has represented it as complete. A narrow exception.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · UNCONSCIONABILITY · IMPERFECT GIFT · EXCEPTION TO MILROY V LORD

Constitution of trusts.front: T Choithram International SA v Pagarani [2001] PC
Constitution of trusts.issue: Whether a trust was constituted where the settlor, as one of several trustees, had declared the trust orally but had not transferred the property to the other trustees before his death.
Constitution of trusts.rule: The Privy Council held that where a person declares himself trustee as one of several trustees, equity will not allow him to resile from that declaration on the basis that he has not transferred to the other co-trustees. The declaration itself constitutes the trust.
Constitution of trusts.application: Thakurdas Choithram declared a gift to a foundation of which he was one of the trustees. He died before transferring legal title to the other trustees. The PC held the trust was constituted: he was simultaneously settlor, donor, and one trustee, and equity would not allow him to recall the declaration.
Constitution of trusts.conclusion: Where the settlor is one of the trustees, the declaration of trust itself constitutes the trust without any further act of transfer. A settlor-trustee cannot rely on incomplete transfer to defeat their own declaration.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · SETTLOR AS TRUSTEE · DECLARATION SUFFICIENT · CANNOT RESILE

Constitution of trusts.front: George intends to give 1,000 shares in Meridian Ltd to a charitable trust. He signs the stock transfer form and leaves it on his desk. Before delivering it to the company or the trustee, he dies. Is the gift constituted?
Constitution of trusts.answer: No. The trust is not constituted. Under the rule in Milroy v Lord [1862], equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. For shares, the appropriate method of transfer requires delivery of a completed stock transfer form and share certificate to the transferee, followed by registration. George has signed the form but not delivered it — he has not done everything within his power. The Re Rose [1952] principle (equity treats a gift as complete once the donor has done all acts within their power) does not assist because George had not yet delivered the form. Absent Pennington v Waine unconscionability, the gift fails and the shares fall into George's estate.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · IMPERFECT GIFT · MILROY V LORD · RE ROSE · DELIVERY REQUIRED

Constitution of trusts.front: Helen executes a share transfer form and delivers it with the share certificate to her brother Ivan as trustee for her niece. The company has not yet registered Ivan as holder when Helen dies. Is the trust constituted?
Constitution of trusts.answer: Yes. Applying Re Rose [1952], where a donor has done everything in their own power to transfer the property, equity treats the transfer as complete in equity from that point, even though legal title does not pass until registration. Helen executed and delivered the transfer form and certificate — she did all that was required of her. Ivan holds legal title as trustee for the niece from the date of delivery. Registration by the company is a ministerial act outside Helen's control. The trust is constituted in equity on delivery.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · RE ROSE · DONE EVERYTHING IN POWER · EQUITY COMPLETE ON DELIVERY

Constitution of trusts.front: Josephine, a solicitor, tells her client Kenneth orally: 'I am giving you 200 of my shares in Orion plc — they're yours now.' Kenneth thanks her and tells his family. Josephine later changes her mind. Kenneth seeks advice.
Constitution of trusts.answer: The gift is likely not constituted. For shares, the appropriate method of transfer is execution and delivery of a stock transfer form with the share certificate: Milroy v Lord [1862]. An oral statement of gift, however clear, does not transfer legal or beneficial title in shares. Kenneth cannot compel Josephine to complete the transfer — equity will not perfect an imperfect gift for a volunteer. The Pennington v Waine [2002] unconscionability exception might be argued — Kenneth has told his family, suggesting reliance — but the courts apply this exception narrowly. Josephine's change of mind is likely effective. Kenneth's best advice is that the gift fails.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · IMPERFECT GIFT · ORAL GIFT INSUFFICIENT · PENNINGTON EXCEPTION NARROW

Constitution of trusts.front: Louis declares at a board meeting of a family trust foundation: 'I am giving £500,000 to this foundation right now.' Louis is himself one of five trustees of the foundation. He signs no transfer document. He dies the next day. His estate argues the gift failed. Advise the foundation.
Constitution of trusts.answer: The foundation has a strong argument that the trust is constituted. In T Choithram International SA v Pagarani [2001], the Privy Council held that where a settlor is one of the trustees and makes an oral declaration of gift to the trust, equity will not allow them to resile on the ground that no formal transfer was made. Louis was a trustee of the foundation and made an unconditional declaration — he was simultaneously donor and trustee. The trust is constituted as against Louis because equity treats him as holding the £500,000 on trust from the moment of declaration. His estate cannot recover it. The foundation should confirm the oral declaration is evidenced (e.g. board minutes) to assist enforcement.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · SETTLOR AS TRUSTEE · T CHOITHRAM · DECLARATION SUFFICIENT

Constitution of trusts.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Nicholas writes a cheque for £50,000 payable to his niece and hands it to her as a gift. Before she can bank it, Nicholas dies. His estate claims the money back, arguing the gift was not complete. His niece's solicitor argues delivery of the cheque constitutes a perfect gift. Who is right?
Constitution of trusts.issue: The Trap: Treating delivery of a cheque as equivalent to delivery of cash, thereby completing the gift at the moment the cheque changes hands. The student reasons that the donor has done everything in their power — they wrote and delivered the cheque — so equity should treat the gift as complete (Re Rose principle).
Constitution of trusts.rule: The Reality: A cheque is not cash. It is a conditional payment instrument — a direction to a bank to pay. Legal title to the money does not pass on delivery of the cheque; it passes only when the cheque is presented and honoured. Before clearing, the drawer (Nicholas) retains legal title and can countermand the cheque or, as here, die. On death, the authority to pay is revoked automatically. The gift is incomplete and fails: T Choithram International SA v Pagarani [2001] and Re Rose [1952] do not assist because delivery of the cheque was not the 'appropriate method' of transfer for money.
Constitution of trusts.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Re Rose requires the donor to have done everything required of them by the specific method of transfer appropriate to the asset. For cash, the appropriate method is physical delivery of banknotes or bank transfer. A cheque is neither — it is a promise to pay, not payment itself. Equity does not treat a conditional instrument as a completed gift.
Constitution of trusts.conclusion: The Key Rule: Delivery of a cheque does not complete a gift. Title passes only on clearing. Death before clearing revokes the cheque and the gift fails. Cash and cheques are not equivalent for constitution purposes.
Constitution of trusts.summary_line: CONSTITUTION · CHEQUE NOT CASH · GIFT INCOMPLETE · APPROPRIATE METHOD · MILROY V LORD

Automatic resulting trusts.front: What is an automatic resulting trust and when does it arise?
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: An automatic resulting trust (Megarry J's classification in Re Vandervell's Trusts (No 2) [1974]) arises automatically by operation of law — without reference to the parties' intentions — when (1) a trust fails (e.g. for uncertainty or illegality), (2) a trust is fully or partially exhausted and a surplus remains, or (3) a voluntary conveyance fails. The beneficial interest 'results back' to the transferor or their estate. ⚠️ The Vandervell classification is academic shorthand — both types share the same legal basis.
Automatic resulting trusts.summary_line: AUTOMATIC RESULTING TRUST · FAILED TRUST · SURPLUS · RESULTS BACK · RE VANDERVELL

Automatic resulting trusts.front: A trust is set up for 'my children who become solicitors'. None of the children become solicitors. Who holds the trust fund?
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: The trustee holds the fund on automatic resulting trust for the settlor (or the settlor's estate if deceased). The trust purpose has wholly failed — no beneficiary can take. The resulting trust arises by operation of law and is not affected by s.53(1)(b) (s.53(2) exemption). The settlor can then deal with the property as they wish.
Automatic resulting trusts.summary_line: FAILED PURPOSE · AUTOMATIC RESULTING TRUST · SETTLOR · S.53(2) EXEMPT

Automatic resulting trusts.front: What happens to a surplus remaining after a purpose trust or appeal fund is exhausted?
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: The surplus results back to the contributors on automatic resulting trust, unless (a) the contributors intended an outright gift (no resulting trust) or (b) a valid cy-près scheme can redirect it (charitable trusts only). Re Gillingham Bus Disaster Fund [1958]: surplus from a public appeal held on resulting trust for contributors — the public had not intended an outright gift. ⚠️ In practice, tracing contributors to repay small sums may be impractical, and a court may approve cy-près or bona vacantia.
Automatic resulting trusts.summary_line: SURPLUS · RESULTING TRUST · CONTRIBUTORS · RE GILLINGHAM · CY-PRÈS EXCEPTION

Automatic resulting trusts.front: A testator leaves £50,000 'on trust to distribute among members of my book club'. The book club has disbanded and its members cannot be traced. What happens to the £50,000?
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: The gift fails because the purpose (distributing among book club members) is impossible to carry out. Where an express trust fails, the beneficial interest results back to the testator's estate under an automatic resulting trust: Re Gillingham Bus Disaster Fund [1958]. The £50,000 does not pass as part of the testator's residuary estate unless the will directs residue to deal with failed gifts. Instead, it is held on resulting trust for whoever takes the estate on intestacy (or the residuary beneficiaries if the will so provides). The cy-près doctrine does not apply as the gift is not charitable. The estate's personal representatives must deal with the fund accordingly.
Automatic resulting trusts.summary_line: AUTOMATIC RESULTING TRUST · FAILED PURPOSE · RETURNS TO ESTATE · RE GILLINGHAM

Automatic resulting trusts.front: Going deeper: The Quistclose trust — competing theoretical foundations
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: A Quistclose trust arises where money is lent for a specific purpose: if the purpose fails, the money is held on trust for the lender (Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd [1970]). Lord Millett in Twinsectra v Yardley [2002] provided the most authoritative analysis, characterising the arrangement as a primary trust for the purpose and a secondary resulting trust back to the lender if the purpose fails. But this analysis has attracted sustained academic challenge. William Swadling ('Orthodoxy', in Swadling (ed), 'The Quistclose Trust', 2004) argued that the resulting trust analysis is legally unprincipled: a resulting trust arises from failure of basis, but the Quistclose arrangement is better explained as a power held by the borrower rather than a trust obligation. Robert Chambers ('Resulting Trusts', 1997) situated the Quistclose trust within his broader unjust enrichment theory of resulting trusts, arguing it responds to absence of basis rather than failure of purpose. Peter Birks disagreed, insisting that the unjust enrichment analysis conflates trust law with restitution. The unresolved tension: is the Quistclose trust a sui generis device, a species of resulting trust, or a proprietary remedy for unjust enrichment? Each answer carries different implications for priority on insolvency.

Automatic resulting trusts.front: Going deeper: Resulting trusts and unjust enrichment — Chambers vs the traditional taxonomy
Automatic resulting trusts.answer: The traditional taxonomy of resulting trusts (Megarry J in Re Vandervell's Trusts (No 2) [1974]) divided them into automatic resulting trusts (arising when an express trust fails or does not exhaust the beneficial interest) and presumed resulting trusts (arising from voluntary transfers or purchase in another's name). Robert Chambers ('Resulting Trusts', 1997) challenged this taxonomy root and branch: he argued that all resulting trusts arise from a single principle — the absence of any intention to pass the beneficial interest to the recipient — and that they respond to what Birks would call 'absence of basis' (a species of unjust enrichment). Chambers' theory was judicially noted but not adopted by Lord Millett in Westdeutsche Landesbank v Islington LBC [1996]. Peter Birks supported Chambers' unjust enrichment framing but disagreed on the precise causative event. William Swadling ('A New Role for Resulting Trusts?', (1996) 16 LS 110) robustly defended the traditional view: resulting trusts respond to property law principles, not unjust enrichment, and collapsing the distinction imports an incoherent remedial approach. The unresolved tension: whether resulting trusts are doctrinal creatures of property law or responses to unjust enrichment has significant implications for their scope, defences, and the conditions under which they can be excluded.

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: When does a presumed resulting trust arise on a voluntary conveyance or purchase in another's name?
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: A presumed resulting trust (Megarry J, Re Vandervell No 2) arises when: (1) A makes a voluntary conveyance of property to B without consideration ('A → B for free'), or (2) A provides purchase money for property placed in B's name. In both cases, equity presumes B holds on resulting trust for A. The presumption is rebuttable by evidence of A's intention to make a gift. Dyer v Dyer (1788): the use 'results to him who pays the purchase money'.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMED RESULTING TRUST · VOLUNTARY CONVEYANCE · PURCHASE IN ANOTHER'S NAME · REBUTTABLE

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: What is the presumption of advancement and to which relationships does it apply?
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: The presumption of advancement is the counter-presumption that a voluntary payment or transfer was intended as a gift (outright). It applies to: (1) father to child (Dyer v Dyer), (2) husband to wife (traditionally — but not wife to husband). S.199 Equality Act 2010 prospectively abolishes the presumption of advancement but is not yet in force. The presumption is rebuttable by evidence. ⚠️ Illegality: the presumption of advancement cannot be rebutted by the payer's own evidence of an illegal purpose: Tribe v Tribe [1996] (limited exception).
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMPTION OF ADVANCEMENT · FATHER TO CHILD · HUSBAND TO WIFE · REBUTTABLE · S.199 EA 2010

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: What did Stack v Dowden [2007] say about the role of resulting trusts in the family home context?
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: Lord Hope and Baroness Hale: the presumed resulting trust (based on financial contribution alone) is not the appropriate analysis for the family home between cohabitants. The correct approach is the common intention constructive trust, which takes into account the whole course of dealing between the parties. ⚠️ The resulting trust analysis still applies to purely commercial contexts and non-domestic property.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: STACK V DOWDEN · FAMILY HOME · COMMON INTENTION CT · RESULTING TRUST SIDELINED

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.issue: Whether a resulting trust arises in favour of the person who provided the purchase money where property is conveyed into another's name.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.rule: Where A provides the purchase money for property conveyed into B's name (or into A and B's joint names), a resulting trust arises in favour of A proportionate to their contribution, unless the presumption of advancement or evidence of contrary intent displaces it.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.application: The court held that the person who pays for property but takes it in another's name retains the beneficial interest under a resulting trust. The presumption is that the payer did not intend a gift.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.conclusion: The foundational authority for the presumed resulting trust. Now read alongside Stack v Dowden and Jones v Kernott for family home cases, where the presumption has been substantially modified. Still applies to commercial purchases.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMED RESULTING TRUST · PURCHASE MONEY · BENEFICIAL INTEREST · PROPORTIONATE CONTRIBUTION

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: Peter pays £80,000 towards the purchase of a property that is conveyed into his nephew Robert's name alone. There is no written declaration of trust. Peter later claims a beneficial interest. Advise.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: Peter has a strong claim. Under the presumed resulting trust principle (Dyer v Dyer (1788)), where a person provides the purchase money but conveys into another's name, a resulting trust arises in favour of the payer proportionate to their contribution. The presumption of advancement does not apply between uncle and nephew — it applies only in specified relationships (father to child, husband to wife). Therefore Peter is presumed to hold an 80% beneficial interest (assuming the property was worth £100,000 or that he paid the full price). Robert can rebut this by showing Peter intended a gift, but absent such evidence, Peter retains the beneficial interest.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMED RESULTING TRUST · PURCHASE MONEY · NO ADVANCEMENT · DYER V DYER

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: Quentin transfers £30,000 into his adult daughter Ruth's savings account, telling her: 'This will help you get started.' Ruth spends £10,000 on a car and keeps the rest. Quentin later claims the money back. Advise.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: Quentin faces difficulty. The presumption of advancement applies between a father and his child (confirmed for adult children in English law), which raises a presumption that the transfer was a gift, not a resulting trust. Quentin can rebut this by showing evidence he intended to retain a beneficial interest — but 'this will help you get started' sounds like an outright gift. If the presumption of advancement applies and is unrebutted, Ruth received the £30,000 beneficially. Quentin has no claim to the £20,000 remaining. He cannot claim the £10,000 spent on the car, which is gone. The courts require clear evidence to rebut the presumption of advancement.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMPTION OF ADVANCEMENT · FATHER TO CHILD · GIFT PRESUMED · REBUTTABLE

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front: Sylvia transfers her flat into her brother Tim's name to avoid a potential judgment creditor, intending to reclaim it once proceedings are resolved. Proceedings conclude without a judgment. Tim refuses to return the flat. Sylvia claims it back on resulting trust. Will she succeed?
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.answer: Sylvia faces a significant obstacle: the illegality defence. Where a resulting trust arises from a transfer made to defraud creditors, the claimant may be precluded from relying on the illegal purpose to found a resulting trust claim. The court applies the 'reliance principle': if Sylvia must plead the illegal purpose to establish her claim (i.e. that the transfer was not intended as a gift), she may be barred. However, following Tribe v Tribe [1996], where the illegal purpose has not been carried into effect (i.e. it was not acted upon — here no creditor was defrauded because there was no judgment), the claimant can withdraw from the transaction and recover. Since proceedings concluded without judgment, the illegal purpose was never executed. Sylvia can rely on this to claim the flat back under a resulting trust.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: RESULTING TRUST · ILLEGALITY · TRIBE V TRIBE · PURPOSE NOT CARRIED INTO EFFECT · RECOVERY

Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.subtopic: Presumed resulting trusts and the presumption of advancement
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Olivia pays the full purchase price for a flat which is conveyed into her teenage son's name. When their relationship breaks down, she claims the flat back on the basis of a resulting trust. You advise her that the presumption of resulting trust arises automatically and the flat is hers beneficially.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.issue: The Trap: Ignoring the presumption of advancement. The student identifies a purchase-money resulting trust (Dyer v Dyer (1788)) and concludes Olivia retains the beneficial interest, without considering whether the presumption of advancement operates to rebut it.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.rule: The Reality: The presumption of advancement applies where a person standing in loco parentis (including a mother, not just a father, under modern law following Equality Act 2010 reasoning and Laskar v Laskar [2008]) transfers property to a child. Where the presumption of advancement applies, the transfer is presumed to be a gift, not a resulting trust. Olivia must rebut the presumption of advancement — not the other way around. The flat is presumed to belong to her son.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The resulting trust presumption (Dyer v Dyer) is the starting point, but the presumption of advancement is a counter-presumption that displaces it in qualifying relationships. The student applies only the first presumption and ignores the second. Both must be considered: the presumption of advancement rebuts the resulting trust presumption in parent-to-child transfers.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.conclusion: The Key Rule: Check for the presumption of advancement after identifying a potential resulting trust. In parent-to-child transfers, the presumption of advancement displaces the resulting trust — the transfer is presumed a gift. Olivia bears the burden of rebutting it.
Presumed resulting trusts and the presum.summary_line: PRESUMPTION OF ADVANCEMENT · RESULTING TRUST · PARENT TO CHILD · BURDEN OF PROOF · DYER V DYER

Common intention constructive trusts.front: What are the two stages of analysis for a common intention constructive trust of the family home?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Stage 1 — Has a common intention to share the beneficial interest been established? This can be express (an actual discussion: Eves v Eves [1975]) or inferred from conduct (financial contributions to purchase or mortgage: Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991]). Stage 2 — Has the claimant acted to their detriment in reliance on that common intention? Without detriment, no constructive trust arises. Stack v Dowden [2007] / Jones v Kernott [2011] (Supreme Court) refined the approach for cohabitants: courts look at the whole course of dealing to impute a fair share.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: COMMON INTENTION CT · TWO STAGES · EXPRESS OR INFERRED · DETRIMENT · STACK · JONES V KERNOTT

Common intention constructive trusts.front: What did Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] say about what conduct can establish a common intention?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Lord Bridge (HL): (1) Express common intention: direct discussions between the parties showing an agreement or understanding that the non-owner will have a beneficial share — even if that agreement is made on a false representation. (2) Inferred common intention: direct contributions to the purchase price or mortgage payments are sufficient; anything less (e.g. DIY, housekeeping) is insufficient on its own. ⚠️ Stack v Dowden [2007] subsequently softened the Rosset threshold, allowing a broader range of conduct to be considered.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: ROSSET · EXPRESS COMMON INTENTION · INFERRED FROM MORTGAGE · DETRIMENT · STACK SOFTENED

Common intention constructive trusts.front: How does Jones v Kernott [2011] allow the court to quantify beneficial shares?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Supreme Court (Lady Hale / Lord Walker): once a common intention to share is established, the court first asks whether the parties had a common intention as to the proportions of their shares. If they did — give effect to it. If they did not — the court imputes what is fair having regard to the whole course of dealing. The court may find that intentions changed over time (as in Kernott, where the shares shifted after separation). ⚠️ Imputation is only available at the quantification stage, not the initial establishment stage.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: JONES V KERNOTT · IMPUTATION · QUANTIFICATION · WHOLE COURSE OF DEALING · CHANGING INTENTIONS

Common intention constructive trusts.front: What is the role of detrimental reliance in common intention constructive trusts?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Once a common intention is established, the claimant must show they acted to their detriment in reliance on it. Without detriment, a common intention alone gives rise only to a bare licence or moral obligation — not an enforceable trust. Detriment can include: financial contributions, significant improvements to the property, giving up employment or career opportunities, or other substantial acts undertaken in reliance on the shared understanding: Grant v Edwards [1986] (CA). The detriment need not be financial.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE · GRANT V EDWARDS · FINANCIAL OR NON-FINANCIAL · REQUIRED FOR TRUST

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Can common intention be express even where no written agreement exists?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Yes. Express common intention is established where there were actual discussions about sharing the property — however informal. Classic examples: promises that 'this house will be yours too', or explanations that the non-owner's name was omitted from the title for a specific reason (e.g. fear of creditors, as in Eves v Eves [1975] and Grant v Edwards [1986]). ⚠️ The promise or excuse itself constitutes evidence of the common intention, even though (or because) it was the reason the legal title was kept in one name.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: EXPRESS COMMON INTENTION · INFORMAL DISCUSSIONS · EVES V EVES · GRANT V EDWARDS · EXCUSE AS EVIDENCE

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Lloyds Bank plc v Rosset [1991] HL
Common intention constructive trusts.issue: When does a common intention constructive trust arise over the family home, and what evidence is required to establish it?
Common intention constructive trusts.rule: Lord Bridge: a CICT requires (1) express common intention (discussions, agreement, assurance) coupled with detrimental reliance by the claimant, OR (2) in the absence of express discussions, an intention inferred solely from direct contributions to the purchase price or mortgage. Indirect contributions (domestic labour, paying bills) are insufficient.
Common intention constructive trusts.application: Mrs Rosset supervised renovations but made no financial contribution. The house was in Mr Rosset's sole name and no express discussion of ownership had taken place. The House of Lords held she had no beneficial interest: her contributions were insufficient to infer common intention.
Common intention constructive trusts.conclusion: Rosset sets a high threshold for CICTs. The restrictive approach to indirect contributions has been criticised as failing to reflect modern family life. Stack v Dowden and Jones v Kernott have since relaxed this in joint-name cases but Rosset remains the leading authority for sole-name cases.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · EXPRESS COMMON INTENTION · DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE · DIRECT CONTRIBUTIONS · SOLE NAME

Common intention constructive trusts.issue: In a joint-name property case, what is the starting presumption as to beneficial ownership, and how may it be displaced?
Common intention constructive trusts.rule: Baroness Hale: where property is conveyed into joint names, the starting presumption is equal beneficial ownership. To displace this, the party asserting unequal shares must show the parties' whole course of conduct — financial and otherwise — evinces a different common intention.
Common intention constructive trusts.application: Ms Dowden had contributed significantly more to the purchase price and outgoings. The parties kept their finances entirely separate throughout a long relationship. The HL held this was an exceptional case: the presumption of equality was displaced and Ms Dowden held 65% beneficially.
Common intention constructive trusts.conclusion: Joint-name cases begin with equal beneficial shares. Displacement requires a holistic examination of the parties' conduct. Cases where displacement succeeds are rare. Contrast sole-name cases (governed by Rosset). Jones v Kernott subsequently extended the court's power to impute shares.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: JOINT NAMES · EQUAL BENEFICIAL SHARES · DISPLACEMENT · WHOLE COURSE OF CONDUCT

Common intention constructive trusts.issue: Where parties' intentions as to beneficial shares have changed since acquisition, how should the court determine shares in a joint-name property?
Common intention constructive trusts.rule: The Supreme Court confirmed that the court first seeks the parties' actual common intention (express or inferred). If no such intention can be found, the court may impute an intention — i.e. attribute the fair and just result — based on the parties' whole course of conduct.
Common intention constructive trusts.application: The parties separated in 1993. Ms Jones paid all outgoings thereafter while Mr Kernott made no contribution. The SC held their intentions had diverged on separation: Ms Jones was entitled to 90% on an imputed basis, reflecting her exclusive financial commitment post-separation.
Common intention constructive trusts.conclusion: Jones v Kernott introduces imputation as a fallback where inference fails. The court may impose a fair result if evidence of common intention is absent. This is a significant expansion of judicial discretion in CICT cases, particularly where circumstances have changed post-acquisition.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · JOINT NAMES · IMPUTATION · DIVERGENCE OF INTENTION · WHOLE COURSE OF CONDUCT

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Ursula and Vincent purchase a house in Vincent's sole name. Before completion, Ursula says: 'I want this to be our home — we're equal partners.' Vincent agrees. Ursula contributes £20,000 to the purchase price. They later separate. Ursula claims a 50% share. Advise.
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Ursula has a strong claim. There is express common intention — the pre-completion discussion ('equal partners') plus Vincent's agreement establishes shared ownership intent. Ursula also made a direct financial contribution (£20,000), constituting detrimental reliance: Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991]. Where express common intention and detrimental reliance are established, a common intention constructive trust arises. The parties expressly agreed 50/50 — so unless subsequent conduct rebuts that specific agreement, Ursula holds a 50% beneficial interest despite the property being in Vincent's sole name.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · EXPRESS COMMON INTENTION · DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE · SOLE NAME · ROSSET

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Wendy and Xavier purchase a home in joint names. Wendy contributed 70% of the purchase price; Xavier 30%. No express declaration of trust was made. They separate. Xavier claims equal shares; Wendy claims 70%. Who is correct?
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: In joint-name cases, the starting presumption is equal beneficial ownership: Stack v Dowden [2007]. The burden is on Wendy to displace this presumption by showing the parties' whole course of conduct — financial and personal — indicates unequal shares were intended. Unequal contributions to the purchase price alone are a relevant factor but are insufficient to displace the presumption in the absence of other indicators of separate financial arrangements or contrary intent. Unless Wendy can show the parties kept finances entirely separate and consistently treated their contributions as reflecting their respective shares (as in Stack v Dowden itself), the court is likely to uphold equal shares. Xavier's position is stronger on the stated facts.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · JOINT NAMES · EQUAL SHARES PRESUMPTION · STACK V DOWDEN · DISPLACING PRESUMPTION

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Yasmin and Zac buy a house in joint names in 2010. They separate in 2018. From that point, Yasmin pays the mortgage exclusively and maintains the property. Zac makes no contribution. In 2024, they dispute shares. Yasmin argues her share has increased. Advise.
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Yasmin may succeed in arguing for increased shares under Jones v Kernott [2011]. The SC held that where parties' intentions as to shares have clearly changed post-acquisition, the court first asks whether a new common intention can be inferred from conduct. Here, Yasmin's exclusive mortgage payments and maintenance since 2018 strongly suggest the parties' intentions diverged on separation. If inference fails, the court may impute a fair result based on the whole course of conduct. The 2018 separation is the pivotal moment.
