The court would likely award Yasmin a share reflecting her post-2018 contributions — possibly 70-90% depending on the total period and amounts involved.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · JOINT NAMES · JONES V KERNOTT · CHANGED INTENTIONS · IMPUTATION

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Abby moves into her partner Ben's house (in Ben's sole name). She gives up her own rented flat, redecorates throughout, and contributes to household bills but makes no mortgage payments. They separate. Abby claims a beneficial interest. Advise.
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: Abby faces difficulty. In sole-name cases, Lord Bridge in Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] held that without express common intention (no evidence of discussions about ownership), a beneficial interest can only be inferred from direct financial contributions to the purchase price or mortgage. Abby's contributions — decorating and paying household bills — are indirect contributions and are insufficient under Rosset to establish common intention. Giving up her flat is detrimental but there must first be a common intention to share ownership before detriment becomes relevant. Unless Abby can point to express discussions with Ben about sharing the property, her claim is likely to fail on the current law.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · SOLE NAME · ROSSET · INDIRECT CONTRIBUTIONS INSUFFICIENT · NO EXPRESS INTENTION

Common intention constructive trusts.front: Going deeper: Common intention constructive trusts and non-financial contributions
Common intention constructive trusts.answer: The persistent failure of English law to recognise non-financial contributions — domestic labour, childcare, relationship sacrifices — in common intention constructive trust analysis has attracted sustained feminist legal critique. Simon Gardner ('Rethinking Family Property', (1993) 109 LQR 263) argued that Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991]'s requirement for direct financial contributions as the basis for inferring common intention is irrationally narrow: it systematically disadvantages those (disproportionately women) who contribute through domestic rather than financial means. Baroness Hale, in her extrajudicial writing and in her judgments in Stack v Dowden [2007], acknowledged this tension explicitly — she noted the law must be capable of dealing fairly with 'the realities of family life', including non-financial contributions, yet the sole-name cases still follow Rosset. The Law Commission (Report No 307, 2007, 'Cohabitation: The Financial Consequences of Relationship Breakdown') recommended a statutory scheme for cohabitants based on contributions rather than common intention. The recommendation was not implemented. The unresolved tension: should the CICT doctrine be reformed to recognise all contributions, or should this be left to Parliament via a cohabitation statute that has been awaited for nearly two decades?

Common intention constructive trusts.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Rachel moves into her partner Sam's house (registered in Sam's sole name). She gives up her rented flat, pays all household bills for five years, redecorates throughout, and tends the garden. They separate. You advise Rachel she has a beneficial interest because her contributions clearly show a common intention to share the property.
Common intention constructive trusts.issue: The Trap: Inferring common intention from indirect financial contributions and domestic labour in a sole-name case. The student reasons that five years of household bills and renovation demonstrate a shared intention to own the property, and that detriment (giving up the flat) seals the claim.
Common intention constructive trusts.rule: The Reality: In sole-name cases, Lord Bridge in Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] held that without express common intention (actual discussions about shared ownership), a beneficial interest can only be inferred from direct financial contributions to the purchase price or mortgage. Paying household bills, decorating, and gardening are indirect contributions — insufficient to infer common intention under Rosset. Giving up the rented flat is detrimental reliance, but detriment is only relevant once common intention is established — it cannot create common intention on its own.
Common intention constructive trusts.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The student applies the Stack v Dowden [2007] 'whole course of conduct' approach, which applies to joint-name cases. In sole-name cases, Rosset's more restrictive framework applies. The two regimes are distinct. Rachel's contributions, however substantial, do not satisfy the Rosset threshold for inferring common intention in the absence of any express discussions.
Common intention constructive trusts.conclusion: The Key Rule: Sole-name CICT requires either: (1) express common intention + detrimental reliance, OR (2) direct financial contributions to purchase/mortgage. Indirect contributions (bills, decoration, gardening) cannot infer common intention in a sole-name case. Joint-name rules do not apply.
Common intention constructive trusts.summary_line: CICT · SOLE NAME · ROSSET · INDIRECT CONTRIBUTIONS · EXPRESS COMMON INTENTION REQUIRED

Strangers as constructive trustees.front: What are the two main heads of liability for strangers who receive or assist with a breach of trust?
Strangers as constructive trustees.answer: (1) Knowing receipt: a stranger who receives trust property — or property which is the traceable product of trust property — with knowledge of the breach holds it on constructive trust. (2) Dishonest assistance: a stranger who assists a trustee in a fraudulent/dishonest breach of trust is personally liable to account as a constructive trustee, even without receiving trust property. The two heads are distinct — liability under knowing receipt is receipt-based; under dishonest assistance it is fault-based.
Strangers as constructive trustees.summary_line: KNOWING RECEIPT · DISHONEST ASSISTANCE · TWO HEADS · RECEIPT-BASED VS FAULT-BASED

Strangers as constructive trustees.front: What is the mental element required for knowing receipt?
Strangers as constructive trustees.answer: The recipient must have 'knowledge' of the breach — but the precise standard is disputed. Bank of Credit and Commerce International (Overseas) Ltd v Akindele [2001] (CA, Nourse LJ): the test is whether the recipient's state of knowledge was such as to make it unconscionable for them to retain the benefit. This is a flexible, conscience-based standard. ⚠️ Debate: whether constructive knowledge (ought to have known) suffices, or whether actual knowledge (or wilful blindness) is required — Akindele left this deliberately open.
Strangers as constructive trustees.summary_line: KNOWING RECEIPT · UNCONSCIONABILITY · AKINDELE · CONSTRUCTIVE KNOWLEDGE DEBATE

Strangers as constructive trustees.front: What is the test for dishonest assistance and how is 'dishonesty' defined?
Strangers as constructive trustees.answer: Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995] (PC, Lord Nicholls): the stranger must have assisted a breach of trust and been dishonest. Dishonesty is objective — would an honest person in the defendant's position have acted as the defendant did? The defendant's subjective belief that their conduct was honest is irrelevant if an objective observer would find it dishonest. Twinsectra v Yardley [2002] (HL) initially introduced a subjective element, but Barlow Clowes v Eurotrust [2005] (PC) restored the objective Tan test.
Strangers as constructive trustees.summary_line: DISHONEST ASSISTANCE · ROYAL BRUNEI · OBJECTIVE DISHONESTY · BARLOW CLOWES · TAN TEST

Strangers as constructive trustees.front: Does dishonest assistance require the primary breach of trust to be fraudulent?
Strangers as constructive trustees.answer: No — Royal Brunei v Tan [1995] overruled the old requirement from Barnes v Addy (1874) that the primary breach must be fraudulent or dishonest. Tan: the primary trustee need not be dishonest — even an innocent breach by the trustee suffices. What matters is the assistant's own dishonesty. This significantly broadened liability: a perfectly innocent trustee's breach can trigger dishonest assistance liability in a third party who facilitates it.
Strangers as constructive trustees.summary_line: ROYAL BRUNEI V TAN · PRIMARY BREACH NEED NOT BE FRAUDULENT · ASSISTANT'S DISHONESTY ONLY

Strangers as constructive trustees.front: Carl is the director of a company that holds client funds on trust. Without authorisation, Carl transfers £200,000 to his wife Diana, telling her the funds are from a legitimate bonus. Diana spends £50,000 on renovation and invests £150,000. The company discovers the breach. Advise the company.
Strangers as constructive trustees.answer: The company has potential claims against both Carl and Diana. Against Carl: he is a fiduciary (director/trustee) who misapplied trust funds. He is liable for breach of trust and must restore £200,000 plus any loss. Against Diana: the company may have a knowing receipt claim. Under BCCI v Akindele [2001], Diana is liable if her state of knowledge makes it unconscionable to retain the benefit. She received the funds believing they were a bonus — if that belief was reasonable and she had no notice of the breach, she may not be liable in knowing receipt. However, her receipt of £200,000 in an undocumented transfer may put her on constructive notice. The company can also trace: the £150,000 investment may be claimed as trust property if traceable. The £50,000 spent on renovation may give the company a charge over the property (Re Hallett; Foskett v McKeown).
Strangers as constructive trustees.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · KNOWING RECEIPT · UNCONSCIONABILITY · BCCI V AKINDELE · TRACING

Other constructive trusts.front: When does equity impose a constructive trust for a secret profit made by a fiduciary?
Other constructive trusts.answer: A fiduciary who makes an unauthorised profit by reason of their position (i.e. exploiting an opportunity that came to them in their fiduciary capacity) holds that profit on constructive trust for the principal. The seminal authority is Keech v Sandford (1726): a trustee who renewed a lease in his own name (after failing to obtain renewal for the trust) held the lease on constructive trust. The rule is strict — no defence of good faith or absence of loss to the principal.
Other constructive trusts.summary_line: SECRET PROFIT · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · KEECH V SANDFORD · FIDUCIARY · STRICT RULE

Other constructive trusts.front: What is the Pallant v Morgan equity and when does a constructive trust arise from it?
Other constructive trusts.answer: Pallant v Morgan [1953]: where two parties agree that one will acquire property on the understanding that the other will have an interest, and the non-acquiring party acts to their detriment in reliance on that understanding (e.g. by not competing), the acquirer holds the property on constructive trust to give effect to the agreement. The trust arises at the moment of acquisition. Confirmed and refined in Banner Homes Group v Luff Developments [2000] (CA): the understanding must precede acquisition.
Other constructive trusts.summary_line: PALLANT V MORGAN · PRE-ACQUISITION AGREEMENT · DETRIMENT · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · BANNER HOMES

Other constructive trusts.issue: Whether a trustee who obtained a lease renewal in their own name, after the landlord refused to renew for the trust, held that lease on constructive trust for the beneficiary.
Other constructive trusts.rule: A trustee who obtains any benefit connected with the trust property holds that benefit on constructive trust for the beneficiaries, regardless of the trustee's good faith or the absence of loss to the trust. The rule is strict and prophylactic.
Other constructive trusts.application: The landlord refused to renew the market lease for the infant beneficiary but agreed to grant a new lease to the trustee personally. Despite this, the court held the trustee must hold the new lease on trust: a trustee cannot profit from their position, even if the trust itself could not have obtained the benefit.
Other constructive trusts.conclusion: Keech v Sandford is the founding authority for the no-profit/no-conflict rule in equity. It applies to all fiduciaries. The strictness of the rule — applied even where the trust could not benefit — is justified by the prophylactic need to deter self-dealing.
Other constructive trusts.summary_line: FIDUCIARY · NO-PROFIT RULE · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · SELF-DEALING · PROPHYLACTIC

Other constructive trusts.front: Eric is trustee of a lease held on trust for Felix. The landlord refuses to renew the lease for the trust. Eric negotiates a new personal lease on the same premises and uses them for his own business. Felix discovers this and claims the new lease. Advise Felix.
Other constructive trusts.answer: Felix has a strong claim. Keech v Sandford (1726) establishes that a trustee who obtains a benefit connected with the trust property — including a lease renewal in their own name — holds it on constructive trust for the beneficiaries. The rule applies regardless of the trustee's good faith or the fact that the trust itself could not obtain the benefit (the landlord's refusal). Eric's position — that the trust could not have renewed — is no defence: Keech v Sandford explicitly addressed this and still imposed constructive trust liability. Eric must account to Felix for the new lease and any profits made from the business conducted on the premises.
Other constructive trusts.summary_line: KEECH V SANDFORD · LEASE RENEWAL · FIDUCIARY · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · NO-PROFIT RULE

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: What are the requirements for charitable status under the Charities Act 2011?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: Section 2 Charities Act 2011: a purpose is charitable only if it (1) falls within one of the 13 recognised descriptions of purposes (s.3(1)), and (2) is for the public benefit (s.4). Both requirements must be satisfied. ⚠️ The public benefit requirement is no longer presumed for any head (including education and religion) — it must be demonstrated in every case.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: CHARITIES ACT 2011 · S.2 · 13 DESCRIPTIONS · PUBLIC BENEFIT · NOT PRESUMED

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: List the 13 descriptions of charitable purposes under s.3(1) Charities Act 2011.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: (1) Prevention or relief of poverty. (2) Advancement of education. (3) Advancement of religion. (4) Advancement of health or saving of lives. (5) Advancement of citizenship or community development. (6) Advancement of arts, culture, heritage or science. (7) Advancement of amateur sport. (8) Advancement of human rights, conflict resolution, reconciliation, or equality. (9) Advancement of environmental protection or improvement. (10) Relief of those in need by reason of youth, age, ill-health, disability, financial hardship, or other disadvantage. (11) Advancement of animal welfare. (12) Promotion of the efficiency of the armed forces, police, fire and rescue, or ambulance services. (13) Any other purposes recognised as charitable under previous law or analogous to a recognised purpose.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: 13 DESCRIPTIONS · S.3(1) CA 2011 · POVERTY · EDUCATION · RELIGION · HEALTH · COMMUNITY · ARTS

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: What does 'advancement of religion' require for charitable status?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: Under s.3(2)(a) CA 2011, 'religion' includes belief in more than one god and a religion which does not involve belief in a god. The purpose must be directed at the public, not a private group. The Charity Commission must be satisfied that the religion promotes moral or spiritual improvement: Thornton v Howe (1862). ⚠️ A religion whose beliefs are harmful will not qualify despite this wide definition. The promotion of a religion must be for the public benefit.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: ADVANCEMENT OF RELIGION · S.3(2)(A) CA 2011 · BROAD DEFINITION · MORAL IMPROVEMENT · PUBLIC BENEFIT

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: Can a trust for the relief of poverty among a private group qualify as charitable?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: Yes — the poverty exception: even a trust for a restricted private class (e.g. 'poor relations' or 'poor employees') can be charitable for the relief of poverty: Dingle v Turner [1972] (HL). This is an anomalous exception to the general public benefit requirement. ⚠️ Outside the poverty head, trusts for private classes fail for want of public benefit: the benefit must be to the public at large or a sufficiently large section of it.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: POVERTY EXCEPTION · DINGLE V TURNER · PRIVATE CLASS · ANOMALY · PUBLIC BENEFIT RELAXED

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: How does the Charity Commission assess whether a purpose is 'analogous' to an existing charitable purpose under s.3(1)(m)?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: The Commission applies a 'development by analogy' approach: a purpose qualifies if it is analogous to (or within the spirit of) purposes already recognised as charitable, or analogous to purposes recognised by the courts before the CA 2011. This ensures the list is non-exhaustive and can evolve. Example: preservation of the environment is now s.3(1)(i) but was previously developed by analogy. The Commission's guidance and the Charity Tribunal decisions shape the scope.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: S.3(1)(M) · ANALOGOUS PURPOSE · DEVELOPMENT BY ANALOGY · NON-EXHAUSTIVE · COMMISSION GUIDANCE

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: A trust is established 'for the advancement of education by providing scholarships to students at Westville Grammar School'. The school is fee-paying and selective. Is the trust charitable?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: The trust falls within the 'advancement of education' head under s.3(1)(b) Charities Act 2011. However, it must satisfy the public benefit requirement under s.4. Following R (Independent Schools Council) v Charity Commission [2012], a fee-paying school can satisfy public benefit, but must provide benefit to those who cannot afford fees (e.g. bursaries, scholarships to the wider public). If the scholarships are exclusively for existing fee-paying pupils without any access benefit for disadvantaged students, public benefit may not be satisfied. If the scholarships are genuinely open to the public (including non-pupils), the trust is more likely charitable. The Charity Commission's public benefit guidance is relevant. Further information is needed about eligibility criteria.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: CHARITABLE TRUST · ADVANCEMENT OF EDUCATION · PUBLIC BENEFIT · FEE-PAYING SCHOOL

Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).front: A trust is established 'for the promotion of moral improvement and the relief of spiritual distress in the community of Riverton'. Is this charitable?
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).answer: This is uncertain. 'Moral improvement' is not itself a recognised head under s.3(1) Charities Act 2011, though it may fall within 'advancement of religion' or 'other purposes beneficial to the community' (s.3(1)(m)). 'Relief of spiritual distress' is closer to advancement of religion but only if tied to a recognisable religious framework. A vague reference to spiritual distress without denominational or institutional connection may not satisfy the 'advancement of religion' head. Thornton v Howe (1862) shows that fringe religious purposes can be charitable if sincere and structured. Both limbs must satisfy public benefit under s.4. The trust is legally uncertain — it may need reformulation to identify a clear s.3 head. The Charity Commission's guidance should be consulted.
Heads of charity (Charities Act 2011).summary_line: CHARITABLE TRUST · ADVANCEMENT OF RELIGION · MORAL IMPROVEMENT · PUBLIC BENEFIT · s.3 CA 2011

Public benefit requirement.front: What are the two aspects of the public benefit test under s.4 Charities Act 2011?
Public benefit requirement.answer: The Charity Commission identifies two limbs: (1) Benefit aspect — the purpose must be beneficial and the benefit must not be outweighed by harm. (2) Public aspect — the benefit must be to the public at large, or a sufficiently large and numerically significant section of it; and must not be confined to a private class. Both limbs must be satisfied and public benefit is not presumed for any head of charity.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: PUBLIC BENEFIT · TWO LIMBS · BENEFIT + PUBLIC ASPECT · NOT PRESUMED · S.4 CA 2011

Public benefit requirement.front: Can a fee-charging school or charity satisfy the public benefit requirement?
Public benefit requirement.answer: Yes, but only if the fees do not make the benefit unattainable by the poor. Charity Commission guidance (following R (Independent Schools Council) v Charity Commission [2012], Upper Tribunal): a fee-charging school must provide a meaningful benefit to those who cannot afford fees — not merely token access. The purpose must not be 'for the benefit of a class defined by ability to pay'. ⚠️ The question is whether the poor can 'realistically benefit', not merely theoretically benefit.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: FEE-CHARGING · PUBLIC BENEFIT · INDEPENDENT SCHOOLS COUNCIL · MEANINGFUL ACCESS · NOT TOKENISTIC

Public benefit requirement.front: Why did a trust for exclusively contemplative religious communities historically fail public benefit, and has this changed?
Public benefit requirement.answer: Gilmour v Coats [1949] (HL): a trust for an enclosed Carmelite convent failed public benefit — the nuns' prayers and intercessions were not a tangible, demonstrable benefit to the public. Private, intangible benefits (intercessory prayer) were insufficient. Post-CA 2011, the position is the same in substance: the Commission requires that a charity's purposes benefit the public in a direct and demonstrable way. Purely contemplative communities that provide no direct public benefit remain at risk of failing s.4.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: GILMOUR V COATS · CONTEMPLATIVE COMMUNITY · INTANGIBLE BENEFIT · PUBLIC BENEFIT FAILS

Public benefit requirement.issue: Whether a trust for a community of cloistered nuns who offered prayers for the public but had no direct public interaction satisfied the public benefit requirement for charitable status.
Public benefit requirement.rule: A charitable trust must confer a tangible, demonstrable benefit on a sufficient section of the public. Indirect or intangible benefits — including intercessory prayer — are incapable of satisfying public benefit because they cannot be proven or measured.
Public benefit requirement.application: The Carmelite nuns lived in seclusion, did not admit outsiders, and their benefit to the public was claimed via spiritual intercession. The HL held this was insufficient: the court could not recognise unverifiable spiritual benefit as public benefit for charity law purposes.
Public benefit requirement.conclusion: Public benefit must be tangible and provable. Trusts for purely contemplative religious communities generally fail to satisfy public benefit. Still relevant post-Charities Act 2011, which codified (but did not relax) the public benefit requirement.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: PUBLIC BENEFIT · CHARITABLE TRUST · CLOISTERED COMMUNITY · TANGIBLE BENEFIT REQUIRED

Public benefit requirement.front: A trust is established 'for the relief of poverty among former employees of Brentwood Manufacturing Ltd and their families'. Is this charitable?
Public benefit requirement.answer: Probably yes, despite the restricted class. The relief of poverty is one of the four recognised heads of charity in s.3(1)(a) Charities Act 2011. Trusts for relief of poverty occupy a special position: the public benefit requirement is applied less strictly because poverty itself justifies charitable intervention regardless of the size of the class. Dingle v Turner [1972] confirmed that a trust for poor employees of a named company can be charitable. The class — former employees and their families — is defined by a personal nexus (employment), but this nexus does not defeat charitable status when the purpose is poverty relief. The trust is likely valid as a charitable trust.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: CHARITABLE TRUST · RELIEF OF POVERTY · DINGLE V TURNER · PERSONAL NEXUS PERMITTED

Public benefit requirement.front: A trust is established 'for the advancement of the sport of polo among members of the Highgate Polo Club'. The club has 120 members, all of whom pay substantial annual membership fees. Is the trust charitable?
Public benefit requirement.answer: Probably not charitable. 'Advancement of amateur sport' is a recognised head under s.3(1)(g) Charities Act 2011, but the trust must satisfy public benefit (s.4). A trust exclusively for members of a private club with high membership fees restricts benefit to a private class rather than the public. The Charity Commission requires that the opportunity to benefit is open to a sufficient section of the public and is not restricted by an unduly exclusive criterion (such as high fees or membership selection). A polo club with restrictive membership and high fees is unlikely to satisfy this. The trust benefits a private class, not a section of the public. It is unlikely to be charitable.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: CHARITABLE TRUST · SPORT · PUBLIC BENEFIT · PRIVATE CLUB · EXCLUSIVITY

Public benefit requirement.front: Going deeper: Charitable trusts and public benefit — regulatory overreach or principled reform?
Public benefit requirement.answer: The Charities Act 2006 (consolidated in CA 2011) removed the presumption that certain purposes (education, religion, poverty relief) are automatically for the public benefit, requiring all charities to demonstrate public benefit afresh. The Charity Commission issued guidance on fee-paying schools that was challenged in R (Independent Schools Council) v Charity Commission [2012], where the Upper Tribunal held that the Commission had overstepped its remit in prescribing how schools must provide public benefit. Gareth Morgan ('Charity Law', 5th edn, 2020) argues that the 2006 reform created regulatory uncertainty without substantive improvement in charitable accountability: the Commission's guidance was developed without a clear statutory mandate and was judicially rebuffed. The Independent Schools Council litigation illustrated the tension between the Commission's expansive view of its supervisory role and judicial deference to charitable institutions' own assessments. Critics argue that the public benefit requirement, as interpreted post-2006, penalises traditional charities (independent schools, religious communities) while failing to provide clear criteria for newer forms of benefit. The unresolved tension: does the public benefit requirement operate as a genuine accountability mechanism, or has its post-2006 application created a politically-inflected standard that the courts have been reluctant to enforce consistently?

Public benefit requirement.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A client instructs you that they wish to establish a trust 'for the advancement of education'. You advise them that educational trusts are inherently charitable and no further public benefit analysis is needed — the trust will be automatically registered as a charity.
Public benefit requirement.issue: The Trap: Assuming that falling within a recognised head of charity (s.3(1) Charities Act 2011) automatically satisfies the public benefit requirement. The student reasons that 'advancement of education' is listed in s.3(1)(b), so charitable status follows without more.
Public benefit requirement.rule: The Reality: Section 4 Charities Act 2011 requires all charities — including those within the s.3(1) heads — to satisfy the public benefit requirement independently. The Charities Act 2006 removed the previous presumption of public benefit for education, religion, and poverty relief. Every charity must now demonstrate: (1) the purpose is beneficial; and (2) the benefit is available to the public or a sufficient section of it. A private educational trust with no public access fails public benefit regardless of its educational purpose.
Public benefit requirement.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Before 2006, public benefit was presumed for the four 'traditional' heads. That presumption was abolished. The student is applying pre-2006 law. Post-2011, public benefit is a two-limb test that applies to all purposes without exception: R (Independent Schools Council) v Charity Commission [2012].
Public benefit requirement.conclusion: The Key Rule: s.3 (head of charity) and s.4 (public benefit) are two separate hurdles. Satisfying s.3 does not satisfy s.4. Public benefit must be independently demonstrated for every charitable purpose post-CA 2006/2011.
Public benefit requirement.summary_line: PUBLIC BENEFIT · s.4 CA 2011 · NOT AUTOMATIC · TWO SEPARATE HURDLES · ISC v CHARITY COMMISSION

Cy-près doctrine.front: What is the cy-près doctrine and when does it apply?
Cy-près doctrine.answer: The cy-près doctrine (from Norman French: 'as near as possible') allows a court or the Charity Commission to redirect charitable funds to an alternative purpose as near as possible to the original, when the original purpose has become impossible or impracticable: s.62 Charities Act 2011. Two limbs: (1) initial failure — the purpose was impossible from the outset; (2) subsequent failure — the purpose fails after the gift has already vested. For initial failure, a general charitable intention must be shown.
Cy-près doctrine.summary_line: CY-PRÈS · S.62 CA 2011 · IMPOSSIBLE OR IMPRACTICABLE · INITIAL VS SUBSEQUENT FAILURE · GENERAL INTENTION

Cy-près doctrine.front: What is the difference between initial and subsequent failure for cy-près purposes?
Cy-près doctrine.answer: Initial failure: the charitable purpose is impossible from the outset (e.g. the charity does not exist at the date of the gift). Cy-près applies only if the donor had a general charitable intention — otherwise the gift fails and results back. Re Rymer (1895): gift to a specific college that had closed — no general intention found, trust failed. Subsequent failure: the purpose was possible initially but later fails. Cy-près applies automatically — no need to show general charitable intention because the gift has already vested: Re Slevin [1891].
Cy-près doctrine.summary_line: INITIAL FAILURE · GENERAL INTENTION REQUIRED · RE RYMER · SUBSEQUENT FAILURE · RE SLEVIN · AUTOMATIC

Cy-près doctrine.front: When is a charitable purpose 'impracticable' for cy-près under s.62 CA 2011, beyond strict impossibility?
Cy-près doctrine.answer: Section 62 CA 2011 expanded the grounds: cy-près applies where the original purpose (a) has been fulfilled; (b) cannot be carried out or not according to the directions given; (c) is adequately provided for by other means; (d) has ceased to be a suitable way of applying resources; or (e) has ceased to be, in law, a charitable purpose. This is broader than the old impossibility test — 'impracticable' includes cases where the purpose is outdated or surplus to requirements.
Cy-près doctrine.summary_line: S.62 CA 2011 · EXTENDED GROUNDS · IMPRACTICABLE · OUTDATED · SURPLUS · BEYOND IMPOSSIBILITY

Cy-près doctrine.front: A testator leaves £100,000 'to the Northfield Animal Rescue Centre for the care of stray dogs'. The Centre closed down three months before the testator's death. What happens to the legacy?
Cy-près doctrine.answer: Because the Centre ceased to exist before the testator's death, the gift fails at the outset (initial failure). The cy-près doctrine can only save a charitable gift on initial failure if the testator had a general charitable intention — an intention to benefit charity generally, of which this gift was a particular expression: Re Rymer (1895). If the testator had only a specific intention to benefit this particular Centre, the gift fails and falls into residue (or intestacy). On the facts, the very specific nature of the gift ('to the Northfield Animal Rescue Centre') suggests a specific rather than general charitable intention. The gift likely lapses unless the court can find broader charitable intent from the will as a whole.
Cy-près doctrine.summary_line: CY-PRÈS · INITIAL FAILURE · GENERAL CHARITABLE INTENTION · SPECIFIC GIFT · RE RYMER

Cy-près doctrine.front: A testator leaves £200,000 'to such animal welfare charities as my trustees shall select'. All charities registered at the date of the will have since merged into a new entity. The trustees wish to distribute to the new entity. Is this permissible?
Cy-près doctrine.answer: Yes. The gift demonstrates a general charitable intention — to benefit animal welfare charities broadly, with selection left to trustees. This is not a specific gift to a named charity but a class gift for a charitable purpose. Where a gift evinces general charitable intention and the specific vehicle fails, cy-près allows distribution to the nearest equivalent object. The merger of existing charities into a new entity does not defeat a general charitable intention: the new entity carries out the same purposes. The trustees may distribute to the merged entity. No court order is needed where the purpose continues to be fulfilled; the trustees should take Charity Commission guidance if uncertain.
Cy-près doctrine.summary_line: CY-PRÈS · GENERAL CHARITABLE INTENTION · CLASS GIFT · MERGER · SUBSEQUENT FAILURE

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: What is the beneficiary principle and why do non-charitable purpose trusts generally fail?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: The beneficiary principle (Morice v Bishop of Durham (1804), Grant MR): there must be somebody in whose favour the court can decree performance — a trust without identifiable beneficiaries is unenforceable. Non-charitable purpose trusts fail because (1) there are no beneficiaries to enforce them, (2) they may be perpetual (violating the rule against perpetuities), and (3) they are administratively unworkable. The trust property would result back to the settlor.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: BENEFICIARY PRINCIPLE · MORICE V BISHOP OF DURHAM · UNENFORCEABLE · NO ONE TO SUE · PURPOSE TRUSTS

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: What are the 'anomalous exceptions' to the beneficiary principle for non-charitable purpose trusts?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: The courts have recognised a limited number of exceptions by concession or long practice: (1) Trusts for the maintenance of tombs and graves: Pettingall v Pettingall (1842). (2) Trusts for the maintenance of specific animals: Re Dean (1889). (3) Trusts for the saying of private masses: Bourne v Keane [1919]. These are described as 'concessions to human weakness or sentiment' and will not be extended: Re Astor's Settlement Trusts [1952]. They must also comply with the perpetuity rules (limited to a life in being + 21 years at common law; now 125 years under the Perpetuities and Accumulations Act 2009).
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: ANOMALOUS EXCEPTIONS · GRAVES · ANIMALS · MASSES · NOT EXTENDED · PERPETUITY RULES APPLY

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: How can a non-charitable purpose trust be validly structured offshore, and what is the Re Denley approach domestically?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: Offshore (e.g. Cayman, BVI): many jurisdictions permit non-charitable purpose trusts by statute with an enforcer mechanism. Domestically: Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969] (Goff J): a trust expressed as a purpose but conferring a benefit on identifiable individuals who can enforce it can be treated as a private trust for those individuals — the purpose characterisation does not defeat it. ⚠️ Re Denley is confined to trusts which benefit ascertainable individuals even if framed as purposes (e.g. maintenance of a sports ground for employees).
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: RE DENLEY · PURPOSE + ASCERTAINABLE BENEFICIARIES · VALID · OFFSHORE ENFORCER · DOMESTIC LIMIT

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.issue: Whether a trust for the purpose of maintaining a sports ground for the benefit of company employees was void as a non-charitable purpose trust, or valid as a trust for ascertainable individuals.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.rule: Goff J: a purpose trust is not void for infringing the beneficiary principle where the purpose is directly or indirectly for the benefit of ascertainable individuals who can enforce the trust. Such a trust is properly characterised as a trust for persons, not purposes.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.application: The trust deed directed land to be maintained as a sports ground primarily for employees of a company. The court held employees were identifiable individuals who could apply to court to enforce the trust — it was a trust for persons sufficiently certain to be valid.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.conclusion: Re Denley carves out an exception to the beneficiary principle for purpose trusts where identifiable individuals can benefit and enforce. It bridges the gap between pure purpose trusts (void) and orthodox trusts for persons (valid). Widely used for employee benefit and recreational trusts.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: BENEFICIARY PRINCIPLE · PURPOSE TRUST EXCEPTION · IDENTIFIABLE BENEFICIARIES · ENFORCEABLE

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: A will leaves £50,000 'on trust for the purpose of maintaining my grave and erecting a suitable monument'. Is this valid?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: Graves and tombs trusts are an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle permitted by statute and recognised at common law. Provided the trust is limited to a perpetuity period (the Royal Lives period or a fixed period no longer than 21 years — though the Perpetuities and Accumulations Act 2009 may allow longer periods), such trusts are valid as a concession to public policy. The trustees (or testator's personal representatives) may enforce it. There is no beneficiary to enforce in the orthodox sense, but the courts tolerate such trusts as a category exception: Re Dean (1889). The £50,000 trust for grave maintenance is valid provided it does not breach the perpetuity rules.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: NON-CHARITABLE PURPOSE TRUST · GRAVES EXCEPTION · BENEFICIARY PRINCIPLE · PERPETUITY

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: A trust deed directs trustees to hold funds 'for the promotion of good relations between nations and the fostering of international understanding'. Is this valid?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: No. This is a pure non-charitable purpose trust and is void under the beneficiary principle: there is no beneficiary who can enforce it. The purpose is abstract and not within any recognised exception to the beneficiary principle (graves, tombs, care of specific animals, monuments: Re Dean; Pettingall v Pettingall). It is also not charitable — 'promotion of good relations between nations' is too vague to constitute a specific charitable head. Re Astor's Settlement Trusts [1952] held a trust for similar abstract, high-minded purposes void: such purposes are not only unenforceable but often uncertain. The funds result back to the settlor under a resulting trust.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: NON-CHARITABLE PURPOSE TRUST · BENEFICIARY PRINCIPLE · VOID · RE ASTOR · ABSTRACT PURPOSE

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: A trust deed provides funds to be maintained and used as a sports ground 'primarily for the benefit and use of employees of Redshaw Engineering Ltd, with any surplus applied to general community use'. Is this trust valid?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: Yes, this is likely valid under Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969]. Goff J held that a purpose trust is not void for infringing the beneficiary principle where the purpose directly or indirectly benefits ascertainable individuals who can enforce the trust. Here, Redshaw employees are identifiable — they have locus standi to apply to court to enforce the trust. The purpose (sports ground) is a vehicle for benefiting persons, not a pure abstract purpose. The 'general community use' element adds width to the class but does not undermine the core validity: Re Denley's principle is satisfied by the primary employee beneficiaries. The trust is valid.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: RE DENLEY · PURPOSE TRUST EXCEPTION · IDENTIFIABLE BENEFICIARIES · ENFORCEABLE

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front: Going deeper: The beneficiary principle — still justified, or ripe for reform?
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.answer: The beneficiary principle — that a trust must have ascertainable beneficiaries capable of enforcing it (Morice v Bishop of Durham (1804)) — has been the subject of sustained academic challenge. David Hayton ('Developing the Obligation Characteristic of the Trust', (2001) 117 LQR 96) argued that what matters is not the presence of beneficiaries but the existence of an enforcer — a person with standing to hold trustees to account. On this view, purpose trusts could be valid if an independent enforcer were appointed. This approach has been adopted in offshore jurisdictions: the Cayman Islands STAR trust (Special Trusts (Alternative Regime) Law 1997) and similar vehicles in the BVI and Jersey permit valid non-charitable purpose trusts with appointed enforcers, without any requirement for individual beneficiaries. England has not adopted an enforcer mechanism in statute, leaving the beneficiary principle intact. The Jersey Law Commission and the STEP (Society of Trust and Estate Practitioners) working groups have discussed whether English trust law should follow offshore reforms. The unresolved tension: does the beneficiary principle serve a genuine functional purpose (ensuring accountability) or is it an arbitrary doctrinal barrier that English law has retained while the rest of the common law world moves on?

The beneficiary principle and exceptions.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A trust is established 'for the purpose of promoting awareness of climate change and encouraging sustainable living practices in the community'. You advise that the trust is valid under Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969] because it benefits the community.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.issue: The Trap: Misapplying Re Denley to save an abstract purpose trust. The student sees a community-benefit purpose and invokes Re Denley as a catch-all exception to the beneficiary principle, reasoning that community members are the beneficiaries.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.rule: The Reality: Re Denley's exception is narrow. It applies only where the purpose directly or indirectly benefits specific, ascertainable individuals who have locus standi to enforce the trust. 'The community' is not an ascertainable class — it is the public at large. A trust for an abstract environmental purpose with no identifiable enforcing individuals remains void under the beneficiary principle: Morice v Bishop of Durham (1804); Re Astor's Settlement Trusts [1952]. If the purpose were charitable (which climate change promotion might be under s.3(1)(l) CA 2011 — environmental protection), it would be valid as a charitable trust, not under Re Denley.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Re Denley saved the sports ground trust because employees were a defined, identifiable group who could go to court. 'The community' has no such definition — it is boundless. Re Denley does not rescue vague public-interest purposes. The student confuses 'beneficial to someone' with 'identifiable beneficiaries who can enforce'.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.conclusion: The Key Rule: Re Denley requires identifiable individuals with standing to enforce — not a vague public class. Abstract or public-at-large purposes remain void. Ask: can a named person go to court to compel trustees to carry out this purpose? If not, Re Denley does not apply.
The beneficiary principle and exceptions.summary_line: RE DENLEY · IDENTIFIABLE BENEFICIARIES · PURPOSE TRUST · VOID · BENEFICIARY PRINCIPLE

Unincorporated associations.front: How can a gift to an unincorporated association be construed as valid?
Unincorporated associations.answer: An unincorporated association has no legal personality, so a direct gift 'to the association' fails. Courts apply four possible constructions: (1) Gift to current members as joint tenants (outright, subject to severance) — valid if the rules allow. (2) Gift to members subject to the contract between them (Re Recher's Will Trust [1972]) — the most common valid analysis. (3) Gift on trust for charitable purposes — valid if charitable. (4) Purpose trust — generally invalid. Construction (2) treats the gift as an accretion to the association's funds governed by the members' contract.
Unincorporated associations.summary_line: UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATION · NO LEGAL PERSONALITY · RE RECHER · CONTRACT ANALYSIS · FOUR CONSTRUCTIONS

Unincorporated associations.front: How is the surplus of an unincorporated association distributed on dissolution?
Unincorporated associations.answer: The surplus belongs to the members at the date of dissolution as joint tenants or tenants in common, with equal shares (unless the rules specify otherwise), distributable in accordance with the members' contract: Re Bucks Constabulary Widows' and Orphans' Fund [1979]. ⚠️ Resulting trust analysis has been largely rejected for this purpose — members who can leave and receive a proportionate share of assets on departure hold assets beneficially, not on resulting trust. Bona vacantia arises only if there are no surviving members.
Unincorporated associations.summary_line: DISSOLUTION SURPLUS · MEMBERS EQUALLY · RE BUCKS CONSTABULARY · NO RESULTING TRUST · BONA VACANTIA

Unincorporated associations.front: What is the significance of the members' contract in the Re Recher construction?
Unincorporated associations.answer: Under Re Recher's Will Trust [1972] (Brightman J), a gift to an unincorporated association is construed as a gift to the current members as an accretion to the funds held subject to the contract between them. The members' contract (rules) determines how the funds are used and what happens on dissolution. The gift is not held on charitable or non-charitable purpose trust — it is beneficially owned by the members collectively. ⚠️ If the contract permits the members to wind up and divide the assets, the resulting trust analysis is defeated.
Unincorporated associations.summary_line: RE RECHER · ACCRETION · CONTRACT BETWEEN MEMBERS · BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP · NOT PURPOSE TRUST

Unincorporated associations.front: Agnes leaves £20,000 in her will 'to the Thornton Birdwatching Society'. The Society is an unincorporated association. It has 40 members, a constitution, and meets weekly. Is this a valid gift?
Unincorporated associations.answer: Yes, most likely. A gift to an unincorporated association can be analysed as a gift to current members beneficially, subject to the association's contractual rules (the contract-holding theory): Re Recher's Will Trust [1972]. On this analysis, Agnes's gift passes to the 40 members as a gift into the common fund governed by the constitution — the members can deal with it as their contract allows, including winding up and distributing among themselves. The association need not have charitable status for the gift to be valid. There is no perpetuity issue because members can wind up at any time. The gift is valid as a gift to members subject to the association's rules.
Unincorporated associations.summary_line: UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATION · CONTRACT-HOLDING THEORY · RE RECHER · GIFT TO MEMBERS

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: What is the statutory duty of care imposed by the Trustee Act 2000?
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: Section 1 Trustee Act 2000: a trustee must exercise such care and skill as is reasonable in the circumstances, having regard in particular to (a) any special knowledge or experience that the trustee has or holds himself out as having; and (b) if the trustee acts in a professional capacity, any knowledge or experience that may reasonably be expected of a person acting in that capacity. Professional trustees (solicitors, fund managers) are held to a higher standard than lay trustees.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: TRUSTEE ACT 2000 · S.1 · DUTY OF CARE · SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE · PROFESSIONAL STANDARD · LAY STANDARD

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: What investment powers do trustees have under the Trustee Act 2000 and what duties apply?
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: Section 3 TA 2000: trustees have a general power to make any investment they could make if absolutely entitled to the trust property ('the general power of investment') — subject to restrictions in the trust instrument. Sections 4–5: when exercising investment powers, trustees must (1) have regard to the standard investment criteria (suitability and diversification — s.4), and (2) take proper advice from a suitably qualified person (unless it is reasonably concluded that it is unnecessary — s.5). Advice must be reviewed periodically.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: TA 2000 · GENERAL POWER · STANDARD INVESTMENT CRITERIA · SUITABILITY · DIVERSIFICATION · ADVICE

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: Can trustees take into account ethical considerations when investing trust funds?
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: Equitable Life Assurance Society v Hyman [2000] and Cowan v Scargill [1985] (Megarry VC): trustees must generally invest to maximise financial returns for beneficiaries — they must not subordinate beneficiaries' financial interests to their own moral views. However, Megarry VC allowed that if all beneficiaries agreed to ethical restrictions, or if the trust instrument authorised them, social investment is permissible. Modern charity law (Charity Commission guidance, 2011) permits charities more latitude to exclude investments contrary to their objects.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: ETHICAL INVESTMENT · COWAN V SCARGILL · FINANCIAL RETURN PRIMARY · BENEFICIARIES' INTERESTS · CHARITIES EXCEPTION

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: What is the standard investment criteria under s.4 TA 2000?
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: Section 4(3) TA 2000: the standard investment criteria are (a) the suitability to the trust of investments of the same kind as any particular investment proposed to be made or retained, and the suitability of that particular investment as an investment of that kind; and (b) the need for diversification of investments of the trust, in so far as is appropriate to the circumstances of the trust. Trustees must consider both criteria before any investment decision and when reviewing the portfolio.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: S.4 TA 2000 · SUITABILITY · DIVERSIFICATION · REVIEW · TWO LIMBS

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.issue: Whether trustees of a pension fund could refuse to invest in overseas or energy companies on ethical and political grounds, prioritising non-financial considerations over beneficiary returns.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.rule: Megarry V-C: trustees must exercise their investment powers in the best financial interests of the beneficiaries. They may not subordinate financial returns to their own ethical or political views. Non-financial considerations are only permissible where beneficiaries unanimously consent or where all options are financially equal.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.application: NUM trustees refused to approve an investment plan that included overseas investments and energy companies, citing political opposition to capitalism and South African apartheid. The court held this was a breach of duty: personal views cannot override the duty to maximise financial returns.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.conclusion: Trustees' investment duty is primarily financial. Ethical considerations may play a limited role only where beneficiaries consent or returns are equal. Now read alongside the Trustee Act 2000 and evolving ESG guidance, but Cowan v Scargill remains the baseline authority.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: INVESTMENT DUTY · BEST FINANCIAL INTERESTS · ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS · NO PERSONAL VIEWS

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: Trustees of a family settlement invest the entire trust fund in shares of a single technology company because one trustee believes the sector will boom. The investment falls 80% in value. Are the trustees in breach of duty?
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: Yes. Trustees are subject to the statutory duty of care under s.1 Trustee Act 2000: they must exercise such care and skill as is reasonable having regard to any special knowledge or experience. The general duty of investment under s.3 TA 2000 requires trustees to invest as a prudent person of business would — this includes the principle of diversification. Concentrating the entire fund in a single company in one sector breaches the duty to diversify: s.4(3)(b) TA 2000 directs trustees to consider the desirability of diversification. The trustees are in breach of their investment duty and are liable to compensate the fund for the resulting loss.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: TRUSTEE ACT 2000 · INVESTMENT DUTY · DIVERSIFICATION · BREACH · COWAN V SCARGILL

Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.subtopic: Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act 2000)
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.front: Trustees of a pension fund wish to exclude all investments in fossil fuel companies on ethical grounds. The potential beneficiaries (all pensioners) are indifferent to the policy. Advise the trustees.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.answer: The trustees' primary duty is to act in the best financial interests of beneficiaries: Cowan v Scargill [1985]. Ethical investment policies are permissible only if: (1) all beneficiaries consent, or (2) two ethical options are equally financially advantageous, or (3) the ethical policy does not harm financial returns. Where beneficiaries are 'indifferent', this falls short of consent — mere indifference is not the same as unanimous consent. However, the trustees must obtain proper investment advice (s.5 TA 2000) to assess whether the exclusion materially harms returns. If ESG-screened investments are demonstrably comparable in return, the policy may be permissible. If returns would be materially lower, the trustees risk breach of duty. They should take specialist advice and document their reasoning carefully.
Duty of care and investment (Trustee Act.summary_line: INVESTMENT DUTY · ETHICAL INVESTMENT · COWAN V SCARGILL · BENEFICIARY CONSENT · ESG

Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.subtopic: Duty to act even-handedly and to inform beneficiaries
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.front: What is the duty of impartiality (even-handedness) and why does it matter?
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.answer: Trustees hold the fund for both income beneficiaries (entitled now) and capital beneficiaries (entitled on remainder). The duty of impartiality requires trustees to balance the competing interests — they must not favour income over capital or vice versa. Classic tension: high-yield income investments (favour income beneficiary) vs capital growth investments (favour remainderman). Howe v Earl Dartmouth (1802): trustees must convert unauthorised investments that are wasting or hazardous to produce a fair balance.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.summary_line: IMPARTIALITY · INCOME VS CAPITAL · HOWE V EARL DARTMOUTH · BALANCING DUTY · CONVERSION

Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.subtopic: Duty to act even-handedly and to inform beneficiaries
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.front: What is the duty to inform and account, and does it extend to disclosing trustees' reasons?
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.answer: Trustees must keep clear accounts and provide information to beneficiaries about the trust and their entitlement: Armitage v Nurse [1998] (Millett LJ). However, trustees are not generally required to disclose their reasons for decisions (Londonderry's Settlement [1965]) — this protects the exercise of a genuine discretion. The beneficiary is entitled to documents that show the existence and administration of the trust (e.g. trust deed, accounts) but not necessarily letters of wishes or deliberative documents.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.summary_line: DUTY TO INFORM · ACCOUNTS · LONDONDERRY · REASONS NOT REQUIRED · LETTERS OF WISHES

Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.subtopic: Duty to act even-handedly and to inform beneficiaries
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.front: Can the rule in Saunders v Vautier (1841) override a trustee's duty of even-handedness?
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.answer: Yes — Saunders v Vautier: if all beneficiaries are of full legal capacity and together hold the entire beneficial interest, they can together bring the trust to an end and call for the trust property. This overrides the trustee's duties, including even-handedness, because there are no continuing beneficiaries to protect. ⚠️ The rule requires: (1) all beneficiaries — none omitted; (2) all legally competent — no minors or those lacking capacity; (3) collectively hold the entire beneficial interest — no other interests outstanding.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.summary_line: SAUNDERS V VAUTIER · COLLAPSE TRUST · ALL BENEFICIARIES · FULL CAPACITY · ENTIRE INTEREST

Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.subtopic: Duty to act even-handedly and to inform beneficiaries
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.front: A trust has a life tenant (Beatrice) and remainderman (Callum). The trustees invest entirely in high-yield bonds, maximising income for Beatrice but depleting capital for Callum. Callum objects. Advise.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.answer: The trustees are likely in breach of their duty to act even-handedly between life tenant and remainderman. Trustees must hold a fair balance between the interests of income beneficiaries (who benefit from high income) and capital beneficiaries (who benefit from capital preservation and growth): Howe v Earl Dartmouth (1802). Investing entirely in high-yield bonds — which maximise current income at the expense of capital — favours Beatrice disproportionately. The trustees should adopt an investment strategy that balances income and capital growth, having regard to both beneficiaries' interests. Callum can apply to court if the trustees refuse to rebalance. The trustees should also consider the total return approach now encouraged under the TA 2000.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.summary_line: EVEN-HANDEDNESS · LIFE TENANT · REMAINDERMAN · HOWE V EARL DARTMOUTH · BALANCE

Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.subtopic: Duty to act even-handedly and to inform beneficiaries
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.front: Eleanor is an adult beneficiary with a vested interest under a trust. She asks the trustees for a copy of the trust deed and accounts. The trustees refuse. Advise Eleanor.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.answer: Eleanor has a right to inspect and be provided with the trust deed and trust accounts. Adult beneficiaries with vested interests have a right to see the documents that define and evidence their beneficial interest. The trust deed establishes Eleanor's rights under the trust. Trust accounts allow her to assess whether the trustees are managing the fund properly. Trustees who refuse without justification are in breach of their duty to inform beneficiaries. Eleanor can apply to court to compel disclosure. The trustees have no basis to refuse the trust deed and accounts to a vested adult beneficiary.
Duty to act even-handedly and to inform.summary_line: DUTY TO INFORM · TRUST DEED · ACCOUNTS · VESTED BENEFICIARY · RIGHT TO DISCLOSURE

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: What is the statutory power of maintenance under s.31 Trustee Act 1925 and when can it be used?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: Section 31 TA 1925: trustees have power to apply trust income for the maintenance, education, or benefit of a minor beneficiary who has a vested or contingent interest in the income or capital of the trust. Conditions: (1) the minor must have a present entitlement to income (or the trust instrument must authorise); (2) the trustees must have regard to the minor's other income and resources; (3) any unused income is accumulated (s.31(2)) and held on trust for the beneficiary. On majority, the beneficiary is entitled to the accumulated income absolutely.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: S.31 TA 1925 · MAINTENANCE · MINORS · VESTED OR CONTINGENT · INCOME · ACCUMULATE SURPLUS

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: What is the statutory power of advancement under s.32 Trustee Act 1925 and what are its limits?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: Section 32 TA 1925: trustees may pay or apply capital money of the trust for the advancement or benefit of any beneficiary entitled to share in capital (vested or contingent). Limits: (1) the amount advanced must not exceed one-half of the beneficiary's presumptive entitlement (as amended by Inheritance and Trustees' Powers Act 2014 — now the full share); (2) advancement must be 'for the advancement or benefit' — broadly construed; (3) if another beneficiary has a prior interest, that beneficiary must consent. ⚠️ The 2014 Act removed the 50% cap — the full presumptive share can now be advanced.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: S.32 TA 1925 · ADVANCEMENT · CAPITAL · HALF SHARE (NOW FULL SHARE) · 2014 ACT · PRIOR INTEREST CONSENT

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: What does 'benefit' mean in the context of the power of advancement under s.32?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: 'Benefit' is broadly construed: Pilkington v IRC [1964] (HL): it covers any use of trust capital that improves the material situation of the beneficiary — including setting up a sub-trust (which itself was the issue in Pilkington, and was held valid). It need not benefit the beneficiary directly or immediately, provided the overall effect is beneficial. ⚠️ Trustees must exercise their discretion honestly — advancement for purposes that primarily benefit third parties may be challenged.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: S.32 · BENEFIT · PILKINGTON V IRC · BROADLY CONSTRUED · SUB-TRUST VALID · HONEST DISCRETION

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: What is the rule in Howe v Earl Dartmouth (1802) and how does it interact with the duty of impartiality?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: The rule in Howe v Earl Dartmouth: where a testator leaves a residuary gift of a mixed fund on trust for income and capital beneficiaries, equity may require the trustees to convert the unauthorised investments into authorised ones (or to apportion income pending conversion) to maintain a fair balance between life tenant and remainderman. Specific investments producing excessive income (wasting assets) must be converted; the life tenant receives a notional income and the balance is held for the remainderman. The rule can be excluded by the trust instrument.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: HOWE V EARL DARTMOUTH · CONVERSION · WASTING ASSETS · INCOME VS CAPITAL BALANCE · EXCLUSION

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: A trust fund of £300,000 is held on trust for Daniel, aged 12, contingently on reaching 25. The trustees wish to apply £10,000 for school fees and £50,000 to purchase a share in a business for Daniel's benefit. Are both payments permissible?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: School fees (£10,000): This is maintenance. Under s.31 Trustee Act 1925, trustees may apply income (and in some cases capital, with appropriate authority) for the maintenance, education, or benefit of a minor beneficiary with a contingent interest. School fees are a paradigm maintenance payment. Permissible, subject to the trust deed not excluding s.31. Business share (£50,000): This is advancement. Under s.32 TA 1925 (as amended by the Inheritance and Trustees' Powers Act 2014), trustees may advance up to 100% of a beneficiary's presumptive share for their benefit. £50,000 is 1/6 of £300,000 — well within the statutory power. Purchasing a business share can constitute 'benefit' if it is a reasonable commercial investment for Daniel: Pilkington v IRC [1964]. Both payments are permissible, subject to the trust deed not restricting or excluding these statutory powers.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: MAINTENANCE s.31 TA 1925 · ADVANCEMENT s.32 TA 1925 · PILKINGTON · BENEFIT · 100% LIMIT

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front: A trust fund of £500,000 is held for Florence, aged 20, contingently on reaching 30. Florence has no other income and asks the trustees to advance £100,000 to help her buy a flat. Her parents (who have a life interest) object. Can the trustees make the advancement?
Powers of maintenance and advancement.answer: Possibly. Under s.32 TA 1925 (as amended), trustees may advance up to 100% of a beneficiary's presumptive share — here £500,000 — for their 'benefit'. Buying a flat can constitute benefit: it is a permanent improvement of Florence's financial position: Pilkington v IRC [1964]. £100,000 is 20% of her presumptive share, well within the statutory limit. However, s.32(1)(c) requires the consent of any person with a prior life interest. If Florence's parents have a life interest and object, the trustees cannot make the advancement without their consent. The parents' objection is a statutory bar. Unless the trust deed enlarges the power of advancement or the court is asked to intervene, the advancement cannot proceed over the parents' objection.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: ADVANCEMENT s.32 · LIFE TENANT CONSENT · PILKINGTON · BENEFIT · STATUTORY BAR

Powers of maintenance and advancement.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A trust fund of £400,000 is held for Thomas, aged 28, contingently on reaching 35. Thomas's mother holds a life interest. Thomas urgently needs £80,000 to establish a business. You advise the trustees they can advance this sum immediately under s.32 Trustee Act 1925 as it is within the statutory 100% limit and is clearly for Thomas's benefit.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.issue: The Trap: Advising on the power of advancement without checking the consent requirement. The student correctly identifies that £80,000 is within the 100% statutory limit (Inheritance and Trustees' Powers Act 2014) and that establishing a business is a legitimate 'benefit' under Pilkington v IRC [1964], and concludes the advancement can proceed.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.rule: The Reality: Section 32(1)(c) Trustee Act 1925 provides that no advancement may be made without the consent of any person with a prior life interest — here, Thomas's mother. Her consent is a statutory precondition, not a courtesy. Without it, the advancement is void regardless of the amount or the merit of the purpose. The trustees cannot pay £80,000 to Thomas without first obtaining the mother's written consent.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The student checks the quantum (within 100%) and the purpose (benefit — correct under Pilkington) but fails to check the consent condition. s.32(1)(c) is the third limb of the power and is non-negotiable. The life tenant's interest protects her income stream — advancing capital reduces the fund from which her income is generated, hence the requirement for her consent.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.conclusion: The Key Rule: Before advising on any advancement, check three things: (1) is it within the 100% limit? (2) is it for the beneficiary's benefit? (3) has every person with a prior life interest consented in writing? All three must be satisfied. Consent is a statutory precondition, not optional.
Powers of maintenance and advancement.summary_line: ADVANCEMENT · s.32 TA 1925 · LIFE TENANT CONSENT · STATUTORY PRECONDITION · PILKINGTON

Delegation.front: What are the rules governing trustees' power to delegate under the Trustee Act 2000?
Delegation.answer: Sections 11–23 TA 2000: trustees may collectively delegate asset management functions to an agent (s.11). They may not delegate their decision-making functions as trustees (e.g. deciding to exercise a discretionary power). When delegating, trustees must: (1) set out the policy statement (s.15); (2) comply with the statutory duty of care in selecting, instructing, and supervising the agent; (3) review the agent's activity at reasonable intervals (s.22). If they comply, they are not liable for the agent's acts: s.23.
Delegation.summary_line: TA 2000 · S.11 · ASSET MANAGEMENT · POLICY STATEMENT · SUPERVISE · NOT LIABLE IF COMPLY

Delegation.front: Can an individual trustee delegate their functions under the Trustee Act 1925?
Delegation.answer: Section 25 TA 1925 (as amended by Trustee Delegation Act 1999): an individual trustee may delegate all or any of their trusteeship functions (including discretions) to an attorney by power of attorney for a period not exceeding 12 months. The delegating trustee remains liable for the attorney's acts unless they exercised reasonable care in selecting and supervising the attorney. ⚠️ Contrast with s.11 TA 2000 (collective delegation of asset management only — no 12-month limit).
Delegation.summary_line: S.25 TA 1925 · INDIVIDUAL DELEGATION · POWER OF ATTORNEY · 12 MONTHS · DELEGATING TRUSTEE LIABLE

Delegation.front: Trustees of a large pension fund delegate all investment decisions to a fund manager, signing a delegation agreement and then taking no further interest in the manager's activities for three years. A bad investment decision results in a £2m loss. Are the trustees liable?
Delegation.answer: Yes, probably. Trustees may delegate investment functions to an agent under s.11 Trustee Act 2000, but must comply with the conditions in s.15: they must prepare a policy statement and ensure the agent complies with it; must keep the agent's acts under review (s.22 TA 2000); and must intervene if necessary. Taking no interest in the agent's activities for three years is a clear breach of the ongoing review duty (s.22). The statutory duty of care applies to the duty to review. The trustees are personally liable for loss caused by their failure to review: Re Lucking's Will Trusts [1968] (predating TA 2000 but establishing the review principle). They cannot shelter behind the delegation itself — delegation does not absolve them of the duty to supervise.
Delegation.summary_line: DELEGATION · s.11 TA 2000 · DUTY TO REVIEW s.22 · LIABILITY · RE LUCKING

Personal liability and causation.front: What is the basic measure of compensation for breach of trust and how does causation work?
Personal liability and causation.answer: A trustee in breach is personally liable to restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in but for the breach — i.e. equitable compensation. The House of Lords in Target Holdings v Redferns [1996] confirmed that: (1) equitable compensation is aimed at restoration; (2) causation is required — the loss must be caused by the breach; (3) the beneficiary must demonstrate that the breach caused the loss, not merely that loss followed the breach. ⚠️ Contrast AIB Group (UK) plc v Mark Redler & Co [2014] (SC): causation must be shown on the facts; no presumption.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · EQUITABLE COMPENSATION · RESTORATION · CAUSATION · TARGET HOLDINGS · AIB GROUP

Personal liability and causation.front: Are trustees jointly and severally liable for breach of trust?
Personal liability and causation.answer: Yes — each trustee who participated in or consented to a breach is jointly and severally liable for the entire loss. A co-trustee cannot avoid liability by showing that another trustee took the initiative: Bahin v Hughes (1886). However, a trustee who was passive (allowed another to act) may still be liable — leaving the management entirely to a co-trustee is itself a breach: Re Lucking's Will Trusts [1968]. ⚠️ The trustee who pays in full can seek contribution from co-trustees under the Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: JOINT AND SEVERAL LIABILITY · BAHIN V HUGHES · PASSIVE TRUSTEE · RE LUCKING · CONTRIBUTION ACT 1978

Personal liability and causation.front: What is the rule in Re Dawson [1966] regarding the measure of liability for breach of trust?
Personal liability and causation.answer: Street J (NSWSC, followed in English courts): a trustee in breach of trust is liable to restore the trust fund in specie — i.e. to replace the actual asset lost, not merely its value at the date of breach. The measure is the full value needed to restore the fund at the date of judgment, including any increase in value since the breach. This is more generous than common law damages (which freeze at date of breach). ⚠️ Target Holdings and AIB Group have since required causation, but the measure of restoration remains broad.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: RE DAWSON · RESTORATION · VALUE AT JUDGMENT · IN SPECIE · BROADER THAN COMMON LAW DAMAGES

Personal liability and causation.front: Target Holdings Ltd v Redferns [1996] HL
Personal liability and causation.issue: Whether solicitor-trustees who paid away mortgage funds in breach of trust were liable to restore the full amount, regardless of whether the breach caused the lender's ultimate loss.
Personal liability and causation.rule: Lord Browne-Wilkinson: in commercial trust cases, equitable compensation for breach of trust requires a causal link between the breach and the loss. The traditional rule of immediate restoration of the trust fund must be applied with common sense — compensation is assessed at trial, not at the date of breach, and must reflect actual loss.
Personal liability and causation.application: Solicitors paid away mortgage monies before obtaining title deeds, in breach of their undertaking. The HL held that although there was a breach, if the property had been worth the loan amount at the date of breach, no loss flowed from it. Causation matters.
Personal liability and causation.conclusion: Target Holdings introduced a causation requirement into equitable compensation for commercial trusts. It rejected the strict 'restore the fund' rule in the commercial context. Refined by AIB Group (UK) plc v Mark Redler & Co [2014], which confirmed a 'but for' test.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · EQUITABLE COMPENSATION · CAUSATION · COMMERCIAL TRUSTS · LOSS AT TRIAL

Personal liability and causation.front: Trustees invest £200,000 in a speculative venture in breach of their investment duty. The investment is worthless within a year. The trust fund was originally £500,000. Calculate the trustees' liability.
Personal liability and causation.answer: The trustees are personally liable to restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in but for the breach. The measure is restoration of the £200,000 lost, plus any investment return that would have been earned had the money been properly invested — assessed at the date of trial (Target Holdings v Redferns [1996], as applied in AIB Group [2014]). The trustees must pay £200,000 into the trust fund plus interest at the appropriate rate. They are jointly and severally liable — the beneficiary can sue any or all of them for the full sum. There is no reduction for the remaining £300,000 of the fund: each breach is assessed independently.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · PERSONAL LIABILITY · RESTORATION · TARGET HOLDINGS · CAUSATION

Personal liability and causation.front: Solicitor-trustees pay away £400,000 of mortgage advance monies to a borrower before obtaining the executed charge, in breach of their instructions. The property subsequently falls in value to £250,000. The lender claims the full £400,000. How is the trustees' liability assessed?
Personal liability and causation.answer: Following Target Holdings v Redferns [1996] and AIB Group (UK) plc v Mark Redler [2014], equitable compensation for breach of trust in commercial cases requires a causal link between the breach and the loss. The question is: what loss was caused by the breach? If the mortgage would have been entered into in any event (i.e. the lender would have proceeded even with the charge properly obtained), the loss is the difference between what the lender received and what they would have received with proper security. The lender received a charge on property now worth £250,000 — so the loss caused by the breach is £150,000 (the shortfall), not £400,000. The full sum is not recoverable unless the breach caused the entire transaction to be different. The trustees are liable for £150,000, not £400,000.
Personal liability and causation.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · EQUITABLE COMPENSATION · CAUSATION · TARGET HOLDINGS · AIB GROUP · SHORTFALL

Contribution, indemnity, and exemption c.front: When can a trustee claim an indemnity from a co-trustee for breach of trust?
Contribution, indemnity, and exemption c.answer: A trustee can claim a full indemnity (not just contribution) from a co-trustee in two situations: (1) where the co-trustee was the sole fraudulent trustee and the claimant was innocent: Bahin v Hughes (1886); (2) where the co-trustee is also a beneficiary who instigated the breach and benefited from it: Chillingworth v Chambers [1896] — the liability of the trustee-beneficiary is first satisfied from their beneficial interest, then from their personal assets if insufficient.
Contribution, indemnity, and exemption c.summary_line: INDEMNITY · BAHIN V HUGHES · FRAUD · CHILLINGWORTH · TRUSTEE-BENEFICIARY · BENEFICIAL INTEREST FIRST

Contribution, indemnity, and exemption c.front: Are trustee exemption clauses effective and what are their limits?
Contribution, indemnity, and exemption c.answer: Yes, to a significant extent.
